EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity during the afternoon as large scale lift was enhanced by a mid-level disturbance pivoting around an upper-level low centered over southwestern Ontario. The combination of enhanced lift, strong instability and deep layer wind profiles supported organized storm structures including bowing line segments that produced areas of damaging winds and severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail (0.75 inch diameter) was reported at the Walker Building on the campus of Penn State University.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the southern Plains on December 13th and intensified as it headed toward the northeast U.S. A coastal low developed along the Atlantic seaboard on December 15th. Warm advection snow developed on the morning of Saturday. December 14th along a stationary boundary stretched across the Mason-Dixon line and dropped an initial 1 to 3 inches of snow. As the low pressure system intensified, it produced moderate to heavy snowfall across portions of the Poconos and the northern tier of Pennsylvania. The highest snowfall of 10 inches was reported one mile west-southwest of Windfall, Pennsylvania in Bradford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 10 inches across Pike County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported a tree down blocking Gilkeson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported downed trees and power lines blocking Stonelea and Hiawatha Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity during the afternoon as large scale lift was enhanced by a mid-level disturbance pivoting around an upper-level low centered over southwestern Ontario. The combination of enhanced lift, strong instability and deep layer wind profiles supported organized storm structures including bowing line segments that produced areas of damaging winds and severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a few large tree limbs near Biglerville. The limbs blocked the passing lane of Arendtsville Road (Route 234).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of intense thunderstorms developed over the north central mountains in Pennsylvania and raced east across Wyoming, Lackawanna, Luzerne, Pike and southern Wayne counties during the late afternoon and early evening hours of Wednesday April 10th. Numerous trees and power poles were snapped as winds gusts to between 50 and 60 mph along this line. There was a few homes that were damaged from trees falling on them and one structure had a roof blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the southern Plains on December 13th and intensified as it headed toward the northeast U.S. A coastal low developed along the Atlantic seaboard on December 15th. Warm advection snow developed on the morning of Saturday. December 14th along a stationary boundary stretched across the Mason-Dixon line and dropped an initial 1 to 3 inches of snow. As the low pressure system intensified, it produced moderate to heavy snowfall across portions of the Poconos and the northern tier of Pennsylvania. The highest snowfall of 10 inches was reported one mile west-southwest of Windfall, Pennsylvania in Bradford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 7 to 10 inches. The highest snowfall total of 10 inches was reported one mile west-southwest of Windfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A week after a flash flood even in eastern Erie County, another heavy rain event came through with 3 to 5 inches of rain. The hardest hit areas were Summit, McKean, and Greene Townships in eastern Erie County. The City of Erie also suffered some flash flooding as a major thruway was cut off by high water. Four hikers were rescued in a gorge as the creek rose quickly, but no injuries were reported. The rain event was caused by short wave trough over the region, with northwest Pennsylvania situated in the warm sector with dew points in the lower 60s. Some overrunning, training, and lift associated with the higher topography in the region supported high rainfall amounts which fell over an 18 hour period. Rainfall from the week before kept the soil conditions wet, and flash flood guidance was as low as 0.65 for 1 hour rainfall during the event. The observed rainfall rates were not notable, however one observer reported over 2/hr at one point when a heavier cell moved over Greene Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home in Millcreek was flooded from water inundating the first floor and then into the basement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms along a cold front swept east across the region the evening of November 17th. This produced severe thunderstorm wind damage across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland. Around 25 thousand homes were without power across the region due to downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported numerous trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms developed along a cold front across Ohio the evening of October 31st. This line moved east during the overnight hours of November 1st and was able to mix strong winds aloft down to the surface. Severe thunderstorm wind damage was reported across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported multiple trees down along route 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down on roadway at Speer Drive and Woodland Drive.

In Garrett county Maryland low level cold air was trapped allowing for freezing rain to accumulate from a quarter to one half inch across the eastern half of the county.

A general 1 to 3 inches of snow fell in a 6 to 8 hour period behind the cold front across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A week after a flash flood even in eastern Erie County, another heavy rain event came through with 3 to 5 inches of rain. The hardest hit areas were Summit, McKean, and Greene Townships in eastern Erie County. The City of Erie also suffered some flash flooding as a major thruway was cut off by high water. Four hikers were rescued in a gorge as the creek rose quickly, but no injuries were reported. The rain event was caused by short wave trough over the region, with northwest Pennsylvania situated in the warm sector with dew points in the lower 60s. Some overrunning, training, and lift associated with the higher topography in the region supported high rainfall amounts which fell over an 18 hour period. Rainfall from the week before kept the soil conditions wet, and flash flood guidance was as low as 0.65 for 1 hour rainfall during the event. The observed rainfall rates were not notable, however one observer reported over 2/hr at one point when a heavier cell moved over Greene Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over the higher terrain near Erie, PA. The runoff from the estimated 1 to 2.5 of rainfall in the basin was sufficient to flood several area roads. The off and on ramps for I-90 at the route 97 exit were shut down for several hours, starting at rush hour. Water depths were up to 6 feet in areas. One motorists had to be rescued after entering the flood waters. Water inundated two nearby gas stations underground storage tanks.

Some minor water problems were reported in some of the buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly moving across the Ohio Valley was the focus for the development of scattered thunderstorms, some of which were severe, through the evening hours of the 16th of April. While cloud cover kept instability weak, some deep layer shear helped maintain some storms to severe levels. Quarter sized hail and wind damage was reported in portions of western Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio, and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported mud and debris flow blocked half of Robinson Town Centre Boulevard near Park Manor Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold northwest flow over the Great Lakes produced significant lake-effect snow in the Warren County snowbelt. This was a continuing lake-effect snow event from January 31, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy lake-effect snow accumulations ranged between 4 and 8 inches over the Warren County snowbelt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow, low-topped pre-frontal squall line advanced eastward across the Laurel Highlands and south-central mountains during the late morning. Although the squall line exhibited little to no lightning due to its shallow nature, the convective enhancement aided the downward transfer of 50-60kt southerly winds (low-level jet) that were present just above the surface. The narrow line of enhanced showers produced isolated wind damage and high wind gusts before weakening as it moved to the east of the Allegheny Front into more stable air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and large limbs along Royer Mansion Road near Williamsburg. The downed trees damaged a fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure systems combined with a high pressure system passing across nearby Canada to bring a winter storm of snow, sleet and freezing rain from the morning into the overnight on the 14th. Snowfall averaged 6 to 12 inches in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley, 3 to 6 inches in Berks County and the far western and northwestern Philadelphia suburbs and 1 to 4 inches in the local Philadelphia area. Ice accumulations averaged one-quarter to one-third of an inch from Berks County and the Lehigh Valley into the Philadelphia northwest suburbs and around one tenth of an inch in the local Philadelphia area and the Poconos. The combination caused extremely difficult traveling conditions as well as isolated power outages. It also was the second consecutive weekend that a winter storm affected Eastern Pennsylvania. Around Philadelphia several SEPTA commuter bus routes were suspended because of icy roads. ||Precipitation types varied location by location with the most snow in the Poconos and the least around Philadelphia.  In the Poconos, snow began between 9 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST on the 14th and fell heavy at times during the late afternoon and evening. The snow changed to sleet late that evening and then to freezing rain before it ended overnight.  In Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the far northwest Philadelphia suburbs, snow began during the late morning on the 14th and fell moderately at times during the mid to late afternoon. The snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain during the middle of the evening. Freezing rain then continued until the precipitation ended between 1 a.m. EST and 3 a.m. EST on the 15th.  In Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, the snow began during the afternoon of the 14th and changed over briefly to sleet and then to freezing rain around 6 p.m. EST. The freezing rain then changed to rain during the mid to late evening and continued until precipitation ended overnight.||Representative snowfall included 12.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 10.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 9.0 inches in Bangor (Northampton County), 8.0 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County), 7.0 inches in Nazareth and Forks Township (Northampton County), 6.8 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 6.5 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 6.3 inches in Palm (Montgomery County), 6.1 inches in Springtown (Bucks County), 6.0 inches in Marshalls Creek (Monroe County), 5.6 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 5.5 inches in Wyomissing (Berks County), 4.5 inches in Hamburg (Berks County) and Sellersville (Bucks County), 4.2 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 4.0 inches in Elverson (Chester County), 3.5 inches in Warwick (Chester County), 3.4 inches in Reading (Berks County), 3.2 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 3.0 inches in Royersford (Montgomery County), 2.8 inches in Hilltown Township (Bucks County), 2.6 inches in Wayne (Delaware County) and West Norriton (Montgomery County), 1.0 inch in Brookhaven (Delaware County) and Center City Philadelphia and 0.3 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.||Ice accumulations included four tenths of an inch in Allentown (Lehigh County), Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Doylestown (Bucks County), three tenths of an inch in Reading (Berks County), one-quarter of an inch in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Bethlehem (Northampton County), two-tenths of an inch in East Coventry (Chester County) and Skippack (Montgomery County) and one-tenth of an inch in Brookhaven (Delaware County).||The winter storm was caused by a series of  low pressure systems, one emerging from the central Rockies and the second forming in the western Gulf of Mexico on the evening of the 13th. They reached Missouri and Mississippi respectively on the morning of the 14th. Meanwhile, a high pressure system with a fresh supply of cold air reached southern Quebec Province, Canada.  While the southern low pressure system weakened, the northern one reached the central Ohio Valley at 4 p.m. EST on the 14th as the center of the high pressure system started to cross the Saint Lawrence River Valley and slowly eased east during the evening and overnight. A new low pressure system was starting to form on the warm frontal boundary in North Carolina. This latter low pressure system started to intensify as the Ohio Valley low pressure system started to weaken. At 10 p.m. EST on the 14th, the former low pressure system reached the lower Chesapeake Bay.  This low became the main low pressure system and passed just east of Atlantic City, New Jersey at 1 a.m. EST on the 15th and eastern Long Island at 7 a.m. EST on the 15th.  The sequence of secondary formation helped to lock in colder air near the surface throughout most of Eastern Pennsylvania.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees along U.S. Route 1 (Kennett-Oxford Bypass) near Oxford and blocked a couple of lanes on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass over the Ohio Valley was broken up on the 10th by a shortwave trough and a surface cold front that came moved through during the afternoon hours. Storms developed ahead of the cold front around noon, and ended with the exit of the cold front through northwest Pennsylvania around 7 pm. The humid and unstable airmass supported intense rainfall rates verified with reports of 0.75 in 15 minutes. These storms moved over an area that was already saturated from weeks of hit and miss storms and floods, and could not support the additional runoff produced during this event. The result was widespread flash flooding as the storms affected every county in the WFO Cleveland county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported numerous road closures due to torrential rain from storms that moved through the area between 330 PM and 430 PM. NWS radar estimates rainfall amounts of 3 to 4 inches fell over the area during that time. A steel bridge along Keepville Road was completely under water and Route 18 was closed. Albion Local Officials requested evacuations near the Albion Boro Park. In Cranesville, just north of Albion, the sewer system was compromised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced marginally severe hail and torrential rains over southeastern Somerset County. The heavy rain lead to localized flash flooding in Larimer Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential thunderstorm rains produced significant, localized flash flooding in Larimer Township. Rain gauge reports indicate over 2 inches of rain fell in less than 1 hour. The flash flooding caused washouts and severe damage to White Oak Hollow Road (TR-828) and Porter Road (TR-402). The heavy rains caused Wills Creek to rapidly rise out of its banks and cover White Oak Hollow Road. The elevated terrain along with the flash flooding contributed to major damage along Porter Road, as a stream of water took out a large portion of the road.

The sequence of secondary formation helped to lock in colder air near the surface throughout most of Eastern Pennsylvania.

A low pressure system moving towards the Great Lakes helped to push a strong cold front across the Ohio Valley in the early morning hours of the 22nd. A shallow convective line of thunderstorms developed along the front, providing for some strong to severe wind gusts. Damage was reported in several locations across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 call center reported a tree blocking the roadway at the intersection of route 322 and Knox Dale Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening. Snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain/drizzle with a glaze of ice topping storm total snow accumulations between 4 and 8 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reports across the northwest part of the county ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

A quick moving Alberta Clipper type low pressure system brought a wintry mix to the local Philadelphia area and periods of snow to the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania on the 17th. Snowfall averaged 1 to 4 inches, with lower amounts in and around Philadelphia. Ice accumulations around the local Philadelphia area were less than five hundredths of an inch. Temperatures rose above freezing around Noon EST and the wintry mix changed over to plain rain. Then during the mid afternoon precipitation changed back to snow and ended during the late afternoon on the 17th. Across the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania, snow began between 3 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST and fell at its steadiest across the Lehigh Valley and Poconos. A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening, with storm total accumulations between 6 and 10 inches.

An upper air disturbance caused a burst of heavy snow to fall across Berks County and the western Philadelphia suburbs during the morning of the 26th. While amounts were not heavy, it coincided with the morning commute and caused multiple accidents and closures of major roadways. Snowfall in Berks County and western Chester County averaged around two inches. Snowfall in northwest Montgomery County, the western Lehigh Valley and the western Poconos averaged around one inch. The disturbance weakened quickly as it moved farther east in Pennsylvania and only a trace of snow fell in Philadelphia. ||Multi-vehicular accidents led to the closures of the Pennsylvania Turnpike and Interstate 78. In Berks County, two westbound crashes around 8 a.m. EST on Interstate 78 closed the westbound lanes for seven hours between the Krumsville and Lenhartsville interchanges in Greenwich Township. Forty-four people were injured and twenty-five required hospitalization. Thirteen people were entrapped within their vehicles. To the south, on the Pennsylvania Turnpike, four separate crashes that involved thirty-five vehicles just east of the Reading/Lancaster exchange closed the westbound lanes at the Morgantown interchange for more than eight hours. The crashes occurred just west of the Interstate 176 exchange in Caernarvon Township shortly after 9 a.m. EST. Two people were critically injured and ten people overall required hospitalization. Elsewhere in Berks County, hundreds of motorists were stranded for hours on the westbound West Shore Bypass in Cumru Township after a series of crashes just before the Lancaster Avenue exit. A rollover crash on the eastbound West Shore Bypass just before the Interstate 176 exit in Cumru Township snarled traffic in that area. There were multiple crashes on U.S. Route 222 northbound between the Spring Ridge Drive and Pennsylvanuia State Route 183 exits in Spring and Bern Townships. In Bethel Township, Pennsylvania State Route 419 was closed after multiple crashes.    ||In Monroe County, a 64-year-old man was killed when his pickup truck crashed at Silver Springs Boulevard and Mountain View Drive in Polk Township. In the Lehigh Valley, accidents occurred on westbound U.S. Route 22 in South Whitehall Township and Bethlehem Township. In Chester County, another accident on the Pennsylvania Turnpike briefly blocked all westbound traffic in Uwchlan Township. Other accidents in Chester County were reported on U.S. Route 30 in Caln Township and U.S. Route 202 between East Whiteland and Tredyffrin Townships.  Other snow related accidents occurred in Montgomery County and along the Pennsylvania Turnpike Extension in Carbon and Bucks Counties.||In the hardest hit areas, snow began falling between 730 a.m. EST and 8 a.m. EST on the 26th and fell heavy quickly, with many visibilities half a mile or less. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow, low-topped pre-frontal squall line advanced eastward across the Laurel Highlands and south-central mountains during the late morning. Although the squall line exhibited little to no lightning due to its shallow nature, the convective enhancement aided the downward transfer of 50-60kt southerly winds (low-level jet) that were present just above the surface. The narrow line of enhanced showers produced isolated wind damage and high wind gusts before weakening as it moved to the east of the Allegheny Front into more stable air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 69 mph thunderstorm wind gust was measured on the Chickaree Summit near the Cambria/Indiana county-line. The thunderstorm winds knocked down trees. Pea size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening. Snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain/drizzle with a over a quarter inch of ice topping storm total snow accumulations between 3 and 6 inches. The mixed wintry precipitation adversely impacted travel especially along the I-81 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow convective line, void of lightning, moved rapidly eastward through central PA during the early morning hours. The line was along the leading edge of a surface wind shift and in an environment characterized by very strong low level wind fields and minimal to no instability. Isolated strong to locally severe wind gusts occurred along the line, with damage reported in Somerset and Elk counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow line of heavy showers with estimated winds near 60 mph brought down utility wires and caused power outages in Stoystown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave and convective low dropping southeast from lower Michigan produced heavy showers and thunderstorms over Somerset County. The upper-air disturbance was embedded within an anomalously moist northwest flow and tapped into a plume of monsoonal moisture that wrapped northeast from the Desert Southwest around the northern periphery of an expansive southern Plains upper ridge. While much of Somerset County received 1 inch of rain during the course of the day, two concentrated areas of locally heavy rain (2-4 inches) occurred in the northwest and southwest corners of the county. The intense rain rates combined with wet antecedent conditions produced flash flooding which eventually transitioned into areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain caused the Laurel Hill Creek to overflow its banks, flooding nearby roads and low-lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southeastern United States low pressure system that formed on a frontal boundary helped produce average snowfalls of 3 to 5 inches from Berks County and the Lehigh Valley southeast through the Philadelphia area and 1 to 3 inches across the Poconos during the daytime hours on the 10th. The heaviest snow fell in the Philadelphia suburbs where the combination of heavier snow and colder surface temperatures were maximized. The snow caused school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings.  Numerous accidents occurred on roadways in southeastern Pennsylvania as it snowed during the morning commute. Precipitation started in Eastern Pennsylvania between 6 a.m. EST and 8 a.m. EST. The snow fell at its heaviest during the morning.  The snow ended between 1 p.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST that afternoon.||Representative snowfall included 5.5 inches in Quakertown (Bucks County), 5.1 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 5.0 inches In Hatfield and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 4.8 inches in Warwick Township (Bucks County), 4.4 inches in Horsham (Montgomery County), 4.2 inches in Warminster (Bucks County) and Abington (Montgomery County), 4.1 inches in Phoenixville (Chester County) and Wayne (Delaware County), 4.0 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), Drexel Hill (Delaware County) and East Nantmeal (Chester County), 3.9 inches in Concord Township (Delaware County), 3.8 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County), 3.6 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 3.3 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 3.1 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County), 3.0 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County), 2.8 inches in Reading (Berks County), 2.7 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 2.6 inches in Mohnton (Berks County), 2.5 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 2.2 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport,  1.0 inch in Summit Hill (Carbon County) and 0.8 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County).  ||The low pressure system responsible for the snow formed along a stationary front in the southeastern United States overnight on the 9th. At 7 a.m. EST on the 10th, it was located over western North Carolina. It proceeded to move east and reached the North Carolina coastal waters at 7 p.m. EST that evening and moved farther east offshore overnight.

Low pressure approached the area from the west during the overnight hours of August 30-31. A west to east band of thunderstorms developed along the associated frontal boundary with severe winds and hail occurring as this band of thunderstorms pushed south off of Lake Erie. Approximately 50 trees were brought down by the thunderstorm winds in Washington Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed reports of strong thunderstorm winds bringing down a swath of approximately 50 trees in Washington Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance caused a burst of heavy snow to fall across Berks County and the western Philadelphia suburbs during the morning of the 26th. While amounts were not heavy, it coincided with the morning commute and caused multiple accidents and closures of major roadways. Snowfall in Berks County and western Chester County averaged around two inches. Snowfall in northwest Montgomery County, the western Lehigh Valley and the western Poconos averaged around one inch. The disturbance weakened quickly as it moved farther east in Pennsylvania and only a trace of snow fell in Philadelphia. ||Multi-vehicular accidents led to the closures of the Pennsylvania Turnpike and Interstate 78. In Berks County, two westbound crashes around 8 a.m. EST on Interstate 78 closed the westbound lanes for seven hours between the Krumsville and Lenhartsville interchanges in Greenwich Township. Forty-four people were injured and twenty-five required hospitalization. Thirteen people were entrapped within their vehicles. To the south, on the Pennsylvania Turnpike, four separate crashes that involved thirty-five vehicles just east of the Reading/Lancaster exchange closed the westbound lanes at the Morgantown interchange for more than eight hours. The crashes occurred just west of the Interstate 176 exchange in Caernarvon Township shortly after 9 a.m. EST. Two people were critically injured and ten people overall required hospitalization. Elsewhere in Berks County, hundreds of motorists were stranded for hours on the westbound West Shore Bypass in Cumru Township after a series of crashes just before the Lancaster Avenue exit. A rollover crash on the eastbound West Shore Bypass just before the Interstate 176 exit in Cumru Township snarled traffic in that area. There were multiple crashes on U.S. Route 222 northbound between the Spring Ridge Drive and Pennsylvanuia State Route 183 exits in Spring and Bern Townships. In Bethel Township, Pennsylvania State Route 419 was closed after multiple crashes.    ||In Monroe County, a 64-year-old man was killed when his pickup truck crashed at Silver Springs Boulevard and Mountain View Drive in Polk Township. In the Lehigh Valley, accidents occurred on westbound U.S. Route 22 in South Whitehall Township and Bethlehem Township. In Chester County, another accident on the Pennsylvania Turnpike briefly blocked all westbound traffic in Uwchlan Township. Other accidents in Chester County were reported on U.S. Route 30 in Caln Township and U.S. Route 202 between East Whiteland and Tredyffrin Townships.  Other snow related accidents occurred in Montgomery County and along the Pennsylvania Turnpike Extension in Carbon and Bucks Counties.||In the hardest hit areas, snow began falling between 730 a.m. EST and 8 a.m. EST on the 26th and fell heavy quickly, with many visibilities half a mile or less. The snow ended around 10 a.m. EST that morning.  ||Representative snowfall included 2.5 inches in South Heidelberg Township (Berks County), 2.3 inches in Reading (Berks County), 2.1 inches in Bernville (Berks County), 2.0 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County) and Mohnton (Berks County), 1.8 inches in Elverson and East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 1.6 inches in Kutztown (Berks County) and West Caln Township (Chester County), 1.3 inches in Slatington (Lehigh County) and 1.0 inch in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Glenmoore (Chester County) and Schnecksville (Lehigh County).

An upper air disturbance caused a burst of heavy snow to fall across Berks County and the western Philadelphia suburbs during the morning of the 26th. While amounts were not heavy, it coincided with the morning commute and caused multiple accidents and closures of major roadways. Snowfall in Berks County and western Chester County averaged around two inches. Snowfall in northwest Montgomery County, the western Lehigh Valley and the western Poconos averaged around one inch. Forty-four people were injured and twenty-five required hospitalization. Thirteen people were entrapped within their vehicles. To the south, on the Pennsylvania Turnpike, four separate crashes that involved thirty-five vehicles just east of the Reading/Lancaster exchange closed the westbound lanes at the Morgantown interchange for more than eight hours. The crashes occurred just west of the Interstate 176 exchange in Caernarvon Township shortly after 9 a.m. EST. Two people were critically injured and ten people overall required hospitalization. Elsewhere in Berks County, hundreds of motorists were stranded for hours on the westbound West Shore Bypass in Cumru Township after a series of crashes just before the Lancaster Avenue exit. A rollover crash on the eastbound West Shore Bypass just before the Interstate 176 exit in Cumru Township snarled traffic in that area. A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening, with a storm total accumulation of 6 inches.

The snow ended between 1 p.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST that afternoon.||Representative snowfall included 5.5 inches in Quakertown (Bucks County), 5.1 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 5.0 inches In Hatfield and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 4.8 inches in Warwick Township (Bucks County), 4.4 inches in Horsham (Montgomery County), 4.2 inches in Warminster (Bucks County) and Abington (Montgomery County), 4.1 inches in Phoenixville (Chester County) and Wayne (Delaware County), 4.0 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), Drexel Hill (Delaware County) and East Nantmeal (Chester County), 3.9 inches in Concord Township (Delaware County), 3.8 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County), 3.6 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 3.3 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 3.1 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County), 3.0 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County), 2.8 inches in Reading (Berks County), 2.7 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 2.6 inches in Mohnton (Berks County), 2.5 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 2.2 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport,  1.0 inch in Summit Hill (Carbon County) and 0.8 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County).  ||The low pressure system responsible for the snow formed along a stationary front in the southeastern United States overnight on the 9th. At 7 a.m. EST on the 10th, it was located over western North Carolina. It proceeded to move east and reached the North Carolina coastal waters at 7 p.m. EST that evening and moved farther east offshore overnight.

Heavy rainfall between 2 and 3 inches combined with cold season hydrological effects produced widespread poor-drainage and small stream flooding across Adams, York and Lancaster counties. Numerous roads were closed and several water rescues were reported. Minor flood stages were exceed on the Swatara Creek at Harper Tavern and the Conestoga River at Lancaster.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain between 2 and 3 inches cause significant flooding across the county. Road closures were reported in East Berlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening, with storm total accumulations between 5 and 10 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening. Snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain/drizzle with a glaze of ice topping storm total snow accumulations between 4 and 8 inches. Two people lost their lives on a snow-covered Sugar Run Road in Allegheny Township, when an SUV slid sideways into the oncoming lane and collided head-on with a truck. The driver and passenger of the SUV were killed, while the driver of the truck sustained serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with a low pressure system emerging from the Gulf of Mexico caused poor drainage as well as some creek flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania on the 27th. The rain also hampered getaway plans, caused many accidents, as travelers were starting their Thanksgiving holiday weekend trips. On the 27th, the rain and low clouds caused two hour arrival delays at Philadelphia International Airport and caused 60 departing flights to be cancelled. Rain began on the morning of the 26th and started falling heavy at times during the late evening of the 26th. The rain continued to fall heavy at times overnight. Lighter rain then continued through most of the afternoon of the 27th before ending. Event precipitation totals averaged 2 to 3.5 with the highest amounts along the fall line from Delaware through Bucks Counties. The heavy rain was caused by a low pressure system that emerged from the Gulf of Mexico on the 26th. The low moved northeast and reached the Carolinas during the evening of the 26th, Maryland early on the 27th and passed near New York City at 7 a.m. EST on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chester Creek at Chester was above its 8 foot flood stage from 518 a.m. EST through 621 a.m. EST on the 27th. It crested at 8.14 feet at 600 a.m. EST.  Event precipitation totals included 2.50 inches in Upper Chichester Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong northwest winds, low humidity levels and unseasonably dry November helped rapidly spread a couple of brush wildfires in the Lehigh Valley during the daylight hours on the 24th. In Forks Township, one brush fire started on the morning of the 24th near North Delaware Drive and Frost Hollow Road. It was under control at 1 p.m. EST on the 24th. In South Whitehall Township, another brush fire was reported at 1245 p.m. EST in an open field near the intersection of Church Road and Walbert Avenue. No damage or injuries were reported from either wildfire. The peak wind gust on the 24th at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was 37 mph. Through the 24th, only 0.87 inches of precipitation had fallen at the airport in the month of November. Normal November precipitation is 3.50 inches. October was also drier than normal in the Lehigh Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain contributed to a fatal accident on the Schuylkill Expressway (Interstate 76) in Philadelphia. About 430 a.m. EST on the 27th, a westbound Audi automobile hit a pool of water and spun out and hit the barrier and stopped. A minivan following stopped in time. But, a tractor-trailer behind it could not stop in time and struck a minivan.  A 47-year-old male passenger was killed and six other people were injured. The westbound lanes of the Schuylkill remained closed between Exit 341 (Montgomery Drive/West River Drive) and Exit 339 (U.S. Route 1 South - City Avenue) due to the accident.  The westbound side of the expressway completely reopened at 3 p.m. EST that afternoon.  On the eastbound side of the Schuylkill Expressway in nearly the same area, poor drainage flooding closed the expressway between Exit 340B (U.S. Route 1 North - Roosevelt Boulevard) and Exit 341 (Montgomery Drive/West River Drive). Cleanup included removing debris from the roadway. Three of the four lanes were reopened that afternoon.  Additional flooding forced the closure of Interstate 76 within Philadelphia at the 26th Street Tunnel after a cab became stuck in flood waters. The rain also contributed to accidents along Interstate 95 and the Pennsylvania Turnpike. | |The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 543 a.m. EST through 1032 a.m. EST on the 27th. It crested at 7.52 feet at 715 a.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 2.32 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 2.28 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to the flooding, a severe thunderstorm caused wind damage in western Chester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down dozens of large tree limbs and trees in and around the Honeybrook Golf Club and the Tel Hai development in Honey Brook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance moved east across Ohio and combined with a moist/unstable air mass to produce a few isolated severe thunderstorms over northwest Pennsylvania during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Tidioute.

This bridge is found on Township Route 363 off Township Route 1020 between the village of Forks and Jonestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the evening hours of December 10th.  The snow showers continued into the 12th before finally dissipating. The snow was heavy at times with visibilities less than one half mile, especially on the 11th.  Snowfall totals from inland Erie County ranged from 6 to 10 inches with most of this falling on the 11th.  Ten inches or more of accumulation were reported south of North East and also in the Colt Station area.  A few accidents were reported.

Ten inches or more of accumulation were reported south of North East and also in the Colt Station area. A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of January 30th, a convective line of showers formed along a pre-frontal surface trough and intensified as it moved east at 50 MPH. While only isolated thunderstorms occurred, the line produced convective wind damage across portions of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. The highest measured wind gusts were 67 MPH at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport in Latrobe, and 62 MPH at the Wheeling, WV airport. Heavy rainfall with this system caused some flooding of roadways over eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 center reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow resumed across northwestern Pennsylvania around midday on December 15th as very cold air continued to move across Lake Erie.  The snow intensified during the evening hours when visibilities dropped to less than a quarter mile and snowfall rates approached nearly 2 inches per hour.  The snow began to taper off around midday on the 16th.   Snowfall totals during this event exceeded a foot in some areas.  Officially, Erie International Airport had a total of 11.5 inches on the 15th and 16th with most of this occurring during the evening of the 15th.  A peak total of 18 inches was reported south of North East with 13.7 inches measured in Mill Creek Township.  Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of deepening low pressure moved northeast across the Ohio Valley on December 14th. The low eventually moved across the southeast corner of Ohio during the evening hours. Snow associated with this low spread into northwestern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of the 14th.  The snow intensified around daybreak when visibilities dropped below a mile. Periods of moderate to heavy snow with visibilities less than a half mile and snowfall rates in excess of an inch per hour occurred during the daylight hours. The snow transitioned to lake effect snow during the evening hours of the 14th with a second period of moderate to heavy snow occurring.  The snow eventually tapered to flurries during the early morning hours of the 15th.   Six to nine inches of snow was reported over much of Erie County.  Officially 7.9 inches was measured at Erie International Airport on the 14th.   The peak in the county was 9.0 inches south of North East.  Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during early morning hours of December 24th.  The snow continued through the evening hours before tapering off.  Intermittent periods of moderate to heavy snow occurred during the daylight hours.  Visibilities were less than one half mile at times and snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour.  Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were common across Erie County.  A peak total of 11.5 inches was reported south of North East with 9.4 inches in Mill Creek Township.  A total of 5.0 inches was reported at Erie International Airport.  Several serious accidents occurred on Interstate 90 causing significant holiday travel delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of deepening low pressure moved northeast across the Ohio Valley on December 14th. The low eventually moved across the southeast corner of Ohio during the evening hours. Snow associated with this low spread into northwestern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of the 14th.  The snow intensified around daybreak when visibilities dropped below a mile. Periods of moderate to heavy snow with visibilities less than a half mile and snowfall rates in excess of an inch per hour occurred during the daylight hours. The snow transitioned to lake effect snow during the evening hours of the 14th with a second period of moderate to heavy snow occurring.  The snow eventually tapered to flurries during the early morning hours of the 15th.   Six to nine inches of snow was reported over much of Erie County.  Officially 7.9 inches was measured at Erie International Airport on the 14th.   The peak in the county was 9.0 inches south of North East.  Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual freezing rain event occurred across portions of Allegheny, Butler, Beaver, and Armstrong counties on the morning of January 9th. Temperatures early morning on the 9th were in the teens, then warmed rapidly ahead of an approaching cold front into the mid and upper 30s. Light rain showers developed well ahead of the cold front, and froze on roadways as road temperatures remained below freezing. Over 100 vehicle accidents were reported across the region. One accident involved 2 fatalities, young boys aged 1 and 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single vehicle accident on icy roads resulted in the death of 2 young boys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began before dawn on December 31st.   Periods of moderate to heavy snow occurred throughout the day.  The snow continued into the New Year with heavy accumulations on January 1st and 2nd as an area of low pressure passed to the south of the area.  Inland areas of Erie County saw 6 or more inches of snow by midnight on the 1st.  Westerly winds gusted to as much as 30 mph during the day.  This caused near whiteout conditions at times along with considerable blowing and drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp cold front plowed across central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours on the 30th. Very strong winds ahead/behind the front were observed with peak gusts around 50 mph. The strong winds caused isolated power outages and minor property damage. Strong wind gusts continued into the afternoon before subsiding by the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew a roof off a structure in Catawissa. The roof came to rest on utility lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front well in advance of low pressure across the Great Lakes produced a mix of winter precipitation across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland. Areas from Oil City to Dubois Pennsylvania received over one quarter inch of ice due to freezing rain, with amounts greater than one quarter inch also reported across Garrett county Maryland. Across the rest of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia up to one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated, with a light accumulation of sleet and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported over one quarter inch of ice accumulation in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight wind field behind an arctic cold front combined with temperatures in the single digits to near zero produced wind chills from 10 to 20 degrees below zero. The lowest readings were during the early morning hours of January 22nd and 23rd across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill readings were around 10 below zero across western Pennsylvania on the mornings of January 22nd and the 23rd. The coldest reported reading was 20 below zero just east of downtown Pittsburgh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the afternoon of November 23rd.  The snow showers continued for about 24 hours and then tapered to flurries during the late afternoon hours of the 24th.  The snow was heavy at times, especially during the evening of the 23rd when visibilities were less than a half mile.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches were common across inland Erie County and northern and eastern Crawford County.  A peak total of 9.5 inches was measured at Titusville in eastern Crawford County.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 35 mph causing a lot of blowing and drifting.  Travel was difficult during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving low pressure center brought a mix of winter precipitation to the upper Ohio Valley. Freezing rain accumulations of over one quarter inch occurred across portions of Tucker county West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland. A light coating of freezing rain mixed with sleet fell over eastern Ohio and the remainder of northern West Virginia. Across western Pennsylvania light mixed freezing rain, sleet and snow produced up to one tenth of an inch of ice with 1 to 2 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light coating of ice up to one tenth of an inch fell across much of western Pennsylvania and the Pittsburgh metro, as well as light snow accumulations of around and inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the late evening hours of November 11th.  Intermittent snow continued on the 12th with the intensity of the snow increasing during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Visibilities were less than a half mile at times with snowfall rates in excess of an inch per hour.  The snow showers tapered off during the early morning hours of the 13th.  Accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were reported over portions of eastern Crawford County.  A peak total of 13.7 inches was reported near Titusville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the late evening hours of November 11th. Accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were reported over portions of eastern Crawford County.  A peak total of 13.7 inches was reported near Titusville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across western Pennsylvania during the evening hours of November 17th.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in advance of this front causing damage in some areas.  Strong non thunderstorm winds also occurred behind the cold front into the early morning hours of November 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed many trees across the central and western portions of Crawford County.  Power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Albert Clipper type system moved across the Ohio Valley spreading light to moderate snow across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland during the 25th. Northwest winds behind the system produced snow showers into the day the 26th. Snowfall for the entire event along and south of a line from Lisbon, Ohio to Pittsburgh and Latrobe, Pennsylvania was 3 to 5 inches over the 2 days. During the 2 days across the higher elevations of northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland and the Laurel and Chestnut Ridges in Pennsylvania 4 to 7 inches fell. Areas from the northern suburbs of Pittsburgh to Interstate 80 in Pennsylvania received 1 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of 3 to 5 inch snow fell across western Pennsylvania from generally from along and north of Interstate 70 to the northern Pittsburgh suburbs. Just over 4 inches of snow fell at the Pittsburgh Airport.

An intense, north-south oriented squall line accompanied a strong cold front across central and eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening hours. The strongly forced low-topped convection (with little to no lightning) developed in an environment characterized by weak instability and high shear. Mini bowing segments/LEWPs embedded within the squall line produced numerous areas of wind damage across the mid-lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires in Mifflintown.

The snow showers were intermittent on the 4th and finally dissipated during the afternoon hours. Snow accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were reported across Erie County.  A peak total of 14.5 inches was reported south of North East.  Officially, 9.2 inches of snow was measured at Erie International Airport on the 3rd and 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept east across the upper Ohio Valley on March 20th. Cold west to northwest winds developed behind the front causing lake effect snow showers to develop. The snow showers were intermittent and finally ended during the afternoon hours of the 22nd. The snow was heavy at times, especially during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st when visibilities were less than a half mile at times. Winds gusted to more than 25 mph causing some blowing and drifting. Snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were reported across much of inland Erie County and northern Crawford County.  A few of the higher totals In Erie County included 18.0 inches south of North East, 17.3 inches at Franklin Center and 17.2 inches near Colt Station.  A peak total of 10.5 inches was reported near Meadville in Crawford County.  A few accidents were reported.

A few of the higher totals In Erie County included 18.0 inches south of North East, 17.3 inches at Franklin Center and 17.2 inches near Colt Station. A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pivoted northward from MD/VA into eastern PA, overspreading a current of moist east-southeast low-level flow along and north of a weak occlusion/surface trough. Modest deep-layer shear combined with a cool pocket aloft associated with the upper low created a favorable set-up for hail. Several locations across the middle-to-lower Susquehanna Valley experienced small to marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of quarter-size (1 inch) hail at 1583 Deiblers Gap Road in Dalmatia. Hail lasted for approximately 20 minutes and varied in size from dimes to quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a low pressure system that moved off the North Carolina coast and an occluded front that moved through Pennsylvania overnight produced pockets of freezing rain over the higher terrain of the Poconos during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. Pockets of dense fog over the higher terrain also occurred with temperatures around the freezing mark. Untreated roadways were hazardous. Ice accretions averaged less than one tenth of an inch. While the Poconos were only near the periphery of the precipitation, a high pressure system passing across the Canadian Maritimes kept sufficient cold air near the surface to have precipitation fall as freezing rain in the higher terrain.

While the Poconos were only near the periphery of the precipitation, a high pressure system passing across the Canadian Maritimes kept sufficient cold air near the surface to have precipitation fall as freezing rain in the higher terrain.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in the evening along a weak outflow boundary.  Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across the West Virginia panhandle and Washington county, Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a couple of trees down in Hopewell Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and complex storm system moved across the Ohio Valley from March 5th into the afternoon of the 6th. Snowfall in Garrett county Maryland, Tucker and Preston counties in west Virginia, and the ridges of Fayette and Westmoreland counties received anywhere from 6 to 12 inches of snow in 12 hours. A heavier band of snow in Pennsylvania brought accumulations from 8 to 12 inches across portions of Allegheny, Washington, and Westmoreland counties, with far southern portions of Butler, Armstrong, and Indiana counties receiving 6 to 12 inches. Elsewhere across across eastern Ohio, Western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia 2 to 6 inches of snow fell.

A strong area of low pressure produced a tight pressure gradient across the upper Ohio Valley with southwest winds gusting over 40 MPH at times. A structure in Uniontown was damaged when strong winds aided in the collapse of a roof.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The front of a building had a roof collapse as strong winds pulled the elevated front exterior of the building down.

A strong upper level trough crossed the central Appalachians and induced an area of low pressure over the Piedmont region of southeastern Virginia/northeastern North Carolina. The low moved slowly to the northeast across the southern tip of the Delmarva Peninsula and off the Mid-Atlantic coast. This storm produced moderate to heavy snowfall to the north and west of its track, with snowfall amounts ranging between 5 and 10 inches across far southwestern and south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 to 9 inches were observed from midnight through the morning hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were observed across the southern part of the county from midnight through the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Tennessee Valley pushed a warm front across the upper Ohio Valley during the early morning into the late afternoon of the 18th. A general light mix of freezing rain, snow and sleet fell across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Due to the quick movement of the front, snowfall amounts averaged from 1 to 4 inches, with average freezing rain and sleet accumulations between one tenth and two tenths of an inch. The exceptions to this were across Preston county in West Virginia and Lawrence county in Pennsylvania, where precipitation stayed freezing rain a bit longer for over one quarter inch of ice to accumulate.

A complex area of low pressure moved northeast along the windward side of the central Appalachians, with secondary development east of the mountains along the Mid-Atlantic Piedmont and Coastal Plain. This system spread moderate to heavy snow across much of south-central Pennsylvania with the highest amounts falling over the Laurel Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged between 6 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two pulse-severe thunderstorm clusters impacted portions of the lower Susquehanna Valley during the late afternoon and early evening. The boundary layer over this region was very unstable with temperatures in the low-mid 90s and CAPE values over 3000 J/KG. Despite the favorable thermodynamic parameters, vertical shear and winds aloft were quite weak. This type of convective environment is indicative of pulse storms that produced localized damaging winds and marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down several large limbs along Route 39 in Linglestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced an EF1 tornado near Coudersport in Potter County. Another isolated storm produced wind damage in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team, along with Potter County Emergency Management Officials, confirmed that an EF1 tornado touched down south of Coudersport during the late afternoon of July 27th. The EF1 tornado touched down just west of Big Moores Run Road in Homer Township and traveled to the northeast producing intermittent damage along a three mile path. The last observed damage was found along First Fork Road about one mile south of Ayers Hill. Damage was confined mainly to trees, however two homes sustained damage from the falling limbs and trees. The most significant tree damage occurred along Big Moores Run Road about 1 mile southeast of the intersection of Black Diamond Road and Big Moores Run Road. At this point, winds were estimated near 100 mph with numerous 12 diameter hardwood trees snapped and sheared-off. Elsewhere along the track, winds were estimated around 80 mph with lesser tree damage, confined mainly to toppled trees with shallower root balls. There were no injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly large area of moderate to heavy rainfall (1-2.5 inches) occurred across eastern Pennsylvania, ahead of an upper-level trough pushing eastward from the Mississippi Valley into the Mid-Atlantic region. Broad south-southwest flow ahead of the upper trough and associated frontal band maintained a strong moisture flux with deep-layer moisture (PWAT) values +2SD above normal, and surface dewpoints in the upper 60s and low 70s. Several rounds of rain fell across the middle Susquehanna Valley early on the 10th, with the final burst of heavy rain producing isolated flash flooding in southern half of Northumberland County during the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding across the lower half of Northumberland County. Several roads were closed due to flooding:  Tharp Street, Trevorton Road, West Walnut Street, West Wood Street, and Bear Valley Avenue in Coal Township; Sixth and Pine streets and Fourth and Mulberry streets in Shamokin, and Mountain Road in Ralpho Township. Carbon Run, which flows north toward Shamokin Creek, overtook its banks near the Fifth Street playground in Shamokin, sending black, culm filled water onto the streets. A water rescue was performed near the Shamokin Area High School on State Street, where crews helped a family of three leave a car stranded by water. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure area over Ohio entering a moist environment over PA with high precipitable water values sponsored a flash flooding episode in the lower Susquehanna Valley region late in the afternoon and evening. Thunderstorms repeatedly developing and training over southern Lebanon County dumped up to 7 inches of rain (7.03 measured at the Lebanon COOP) over several hours leading to flooded streams and creeks, roads, and basements especially in and around the City of Lebanon. The flash flooding stranded vehicles and prompted several water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from training thunderstorms produced localized flash flooding across portions of south-central York County. Some locations that were impacted include but are not limited to the city of York and Spring Garden and North Hopewell Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with very heavy rain during the late afternoon and early evening on the 27th caused flooding in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs that lasted overnight.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates as well as Skywarn Spotter and mesonet reports reached 2 to 3 inches in the hardest hit parts of Chester and Bucks Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding mainly in the western half of Chester County. It also led to flooding along sections of the East and West Branches of the Brandywine Creek.  The West Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Honey Brook had moderate flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage from 707 p.m. EDT on the 27th through 218 a.m. EDT on the 28th. It crested at 9.01 feet at 1015 p.m. EDT on the 27th. Farther downstream, the West Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Coatesville was briefly above its 7 foot flood stage from 818 p.m. EDt through 904 p.m. EDT on the 27th. It crested at 7.04 feet at 845 p.m. EDT. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek below Downingtown had moderate flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage from 945 p.m. EDT on the 27th through 129 a.m. EDT on the 28th. It crested at 8.40 feet at 1145 p.m. EDT on the 27th.  Event precipitation totals included 2.36 inches in Modena, 2.27 inches in West Grove and 2.01 inches in Glenmoore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across northwest Pennsylvania during the evening hours on June 25th.  Pennsylvania was located on the periphery of high pressure over the southeastern states. Moisture increased from the southwest around the ridge with dewpoints increasing to near 70 degrees. An unstable airmass developed during the afternoon as temperatures rose into the mid 80s with mixed layer CAPE values near 2000 j/kg. Energy moving through the flow aloft helped to initiate convection and provided the shear necessary for damaging winds across Crawford County, Pennsylvania.  Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines reported across the county, especially in Linesville, Meadville, and Spartansburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operated estimated wind gusts to 70 mph with reports of multiple trees and powerlines down in Linesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure area over Ohio entering a moist environment over PA with high precipitable water values sponsored a flash flooding episode in the lower Susquehanna Valley region late in the afternoon and evening. Thunderstorms repeatedly developing and training over southern Lebanon County dumped up to 7 inches of rain (7.03 measured at the Lebanon COOP) over several hours leading to flooded streams and creeks, roads, and basements especially in and around the City of Lebanon. The flash flooding stranded vehicles and prompted several water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record heavy rain event affected southern Lebanon County during the late afternoon and evening hours. Most of the rain fell in a 3-hour period, resulting in significant flash flooding. The estimated 3-hour rainfall rates exceeded the value for a 1000-year return period. The Quittapahilla Creek was overwhelmed by the intense rainfall, rising quickly and overflowing its banks in the city of Lebanon. The Quittapahilla Creek is a 16.8-mile-long tributary of Swatara Creek.||The pattern over much of the Mid-Atlantic region contained all of the ingredients for a significant heavy rainfall event, including above normal moisture (PWAT) and strong low-level southerly flow. Moderate instability was also a factor early in the event, with CAPE values above 1200 J/KG supporting deep moist convection.||Numerous water rescues were conducted across southern Lebanon County. The Townships that experienced the greatest flooding impacts included Londonderry, Annville, Cornwall, Lebanon, Heidelburg and Millcreek. The flash flooding transitioned into areal flooding/standing water by later in the evening and lasted into the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage in McKean County after midnight. A weak shortwave moved eastward across eastern KY during the predawn hours into the early morning, preceded by a field of large-scale lift and deep moisture transport focused into the lower Susquehanna Valley. Although storm motions were relatively quick, areas of training combined with 2+ precipitable water values, intense precipitation rates and seasonably high stream flows lead to flash flooding and road closures. In addition, low instability coupled with modest low-level shear produced a few supercell storm-structures and isolated damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a few trees west of Bradford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two pulse-severe thunderstorm clusters impacted portions of the lower Susquehanna Valley during the late afternoon and early evening. The boundary layer over this region was very unstable with temperatures in the low-mid 90s and CAPE values over 3000 J/KG. Despite the favorable thermodynamic parameters, vertical shear and winds aloft were quite weak. This type of convective environment is indicative of pulse storms that produced localized damaging winds and marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail (1 inch diameter) was reported along Route 39 in Linglestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected Eastern Pennsylvania from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only around 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 109 degrees in southeastern Pennsylvania and Berks County and 100 to 105 degrees elsewhere. The highest hourly heat index in Philadelphia was 107 degrees. There were two heat related deaths, both in Philadelphia. A 57-year-old male and a female died. While the 57-year-old male had an air conditioner in his home, it was not on when he was found. Four roof workers suffered heat exhaustion while working on the roof of the Chichester Middle School in Delaware County, three had to be hospitalized.| |To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone. Montgomery County issued a code red. Delaware County also provided fans to senior citizens.||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports, 96 degrees in Reading (Berks County), West Chester (Chester County) and Fork Township (Northampton County), 95 degrees in Doylestown and Quakertown in Bucks County and 94 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County). ||A stalling front brought an end to the excessive heat on the 21st and to 90 degree high temperatures by the 22nd.

Significant instability combined with modest lapse rates and abundant deep-layer moisture (PW) produced numerous thunderstorm clusters with locally heavy rain during the afternoon and evening hours across central PA. Although vertical wind shear profiles were not particularly strong, strong destabilization lead to robust updrafts which produced marginally severe hail and damaging winds/wet microbursts. Localized flash flooding occurred in Potter and McKean Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along Route 11 near Shippensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that moved across southeast Pennsylvania during the day on the 10th caused renewed flooding in the area. Event precipitation totals averaged between 1.5 and 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell during the 10th led to flash flooding of roadways and gaged streams and creeks in Chester County. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 834 a.m. EDT through 124 p.m. EDT on the 10th, and it crested at 8.67 feet at 1130 a.m. EDT. The French Creek at Phoenixville was above its 8 foot flood stage from 725 a.m. EDT through 757 a.m. EDT on the 10th, and it crested at 8.05 feet at 745 a.m. EDT. The Valley Creek at Valley Forge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 610 p.m. EDT through 858 p.m. EDT on the 10th. It crested at 7.77 feet at 700 p.m. EDT. Several roads were closed in Chester County due to flooding, including PA Route 926 and Westtown Road in Westtown Township. Creek Road was also closed by PennDot for a time due to flooding. Event precipitation totals included 3.01 inches in Modena, 2.24 inches in West Chester, 2.14 inches in Valley Forge, and 2.12 inches in Northbrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms tied to a surface front  dropped several inches of rain over northwest Pennsylvania the morning of the 27th. Particularly hit hard were Jefferson and adjacent Clearfield counties, were roads were made impassable by over four to six inches of rainfall. The local EMA Director stated there were 49 water rescues and 50 people were placed in shelters. The hardest hit areas include Punxsutawney, Sykesville, Stump Creek, Big Run. and Reynoldsville. Preliminary damage reports have a total of 347 homes and 34 businesses damaged by flood waters. Of those, nine homes were destroyed and three businesses were a total loss. Damage is already estimated to be about $3 million but could be upwards of $5 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 6.00 inches of rain, since rain began this morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported road and basement flooding near route 119.
