EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance combined with  a hot and humid air mass to produce strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the second half of the afternoon and early evening on the 24th. In addition to locally damaging winds, lightning strikes caused one injury and damaged several homes. There were numerous reports of hail with the thunderstorms as the freezing level aloft was relatively low in height above the ground. Most of the hail was small (half an inch or less in diameter).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped quarter size hail in Nazareth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that developed along a cold frontal boundary brought bands of showers and thunderstorms with heavy downpours to Delaware and Chester Counties on the 12th. This caused flooding. Event precipitation totals reached between 2.0 and 4.5 inches across central Delaware County and southern Chester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding in southern Chester County. Event precipitation totals included 3.15 inches in West Grove, 2.86 inches in Landenburg, 2.55 inches in Nottingham and 2.36 inches in Unionville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance combined with  a hot and humid air mass to produce strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the second half of the afternoon and early evening on the 24th. In addition to locally damaging winds, lightning strikes caused one injury and damaged several homes. There were numerous reports of hail with the thunderstorms as the freezing level aloft was relatively low in height above the ground. Most of the hail was small (half an inch or less in diameter).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down or snapped several trees along Stateline Road in Bethel Township. Some of the trees were over 100 years old. One downed tree damaged a home on the road, but no injuries were reported.

Lightning struck and started a fire in the basement and also traveled along the electrical wiring within the house.  The furniture, electronics and clothing were all damaged within the home. The unharmed family was expected to be displaced for about six months.

Thunderstorms developed during the late morning over western Pennsylvania and intensified into a well-defined squall line /QLCS/ with bowing segments as it moved through central Pennsylvania during the afternoon. The line of storms produced corridors of severe straight-line winds which prompted numerous reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along Rt 144 in Germania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms tied to a surface front  dropped several inches of rain over northwest Pennsylvania the morning of the 27th. Particularly hit hard were Jefferson and adjacent Clearfield counties, were roads were made impassable by over four to six inches of rainfall. The local EMA Director stated there were 49 water rescues and 50 people were placed in shelters. The hardest hit areas include Punxsutawney, Sykesville, Stump Creek, Big Run. and Reynoldsville. Preliminary damage reports have a total of 347 homes and 34 businesses damaged by flood waters. Of those, nine homes were destroyed and three businesses were a total loss. Damage is already estimated to be about $3 million but could be upwards of $5 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Department of Highways reported that State Route 322 at Soldier Run was closed due to flooding.

The local EMA Director stated there were 49 water rescues and 50 people were placed in shelters. The hardest hit areas include Punxsutawney, Sykesville, Stump Creek, Big Run. and Reynoldsville. Preliminary damage reports have a total of 347 homes and 34 businesses damaged by flood waters. Of those, nine homes were destroyed and three businesses were a total loss. Damage is already estimated to be about $3 million but could be upwards of $5 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported that State Route 310 was closed near Anita.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported a large tree fell on a moving vehicle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported flooding throughout the town of Reynoldsville. Major arteries in and out of the town were made impassable. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage. Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from early morning thunderstorms struck a residential building along the 800 block of Blooming Glen Road in Hilltown Township at 215 a.m. EDT on the 23rd.  The lightning strike started a fire on the third floor and the roof.  All residents of the building escaped without injury.  The residents were expected to be displaced for about two to five months due to the damaged roof and third floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage. Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of gusty thunderstorm winds and heavy rain brought down a few trees in West Chester Township and caused some damage to yards and patio furniture around 120 a.m. EDT on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected Eastern Pennsylvania from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only around 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 109 degrees in southeastern Pennsylvania and Berks County and 100 to 105 degrees elsewhere. The highest hourly heat index in Philadelphia was 107 degrees. There were two heat related deaths, both in Philadelphia. A 57-year-old male and a female died. While the 57-year-old male had an air conditioner in his home, it was not on when he was found. Four roof workers suffered heat exhaustion while working on the roof of the Chichester Middle School in Delaware County, three had to be hospitalized.| |To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone. Montgomery County issued a code red. Delaware County also provided fans to senior citizens.||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports, 96 degrees in Reading (Berks County), West Chester (Chester County) and Fork Township (Northampton County), 95 degrees in Doylestown and Quakertown in Bucks County and 94 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County). ||A stalling front brought an end to the excessive heat on the 21st and to 90 degree high temperatures by the 22nd.

A weakening low pressure system and a dissipating frontal boundary still managed to produce heavy downpours during the second half of the afternoon and first half of the evening across sections of Bucks and Montgomery Counties. This caused poor drainage and roadway flooding.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 1.0 to 2.5 inches in sections of Bucks and Montgomery Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused street and poor drainage flooding in North Wales. A CoCoRahs observer measured 0.97 inches of rain within a half hour and have an event total of 0.99 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late morning over western Pennsylvania and intensified into a well-defined squall line /QLCS/ with bowing segments as it moved through central Pennsylvania during the afternoon. The line of storms produced corridors of severe straight-line winds which prompted numerous reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Milroy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in Philadelphia and Delaware Counties and also in parts of the Poconos and Lehigh Valley. Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of the Lehigh Valley, 6 to 8 inches in parts of the Poconos and around 8 inches in parts of the local Philadelphia area.  The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event. This event also helped establish back-to-back all-time monthly rainfall records in Philadelphia. The July 2013 precipitation total of 13.24 inches was a new monthly record for Philadelphia and came on the heels of a record breaking 10.56 inches falling in June. The current normal meteorological summer seasonal precipitation average for Philadelphia is 11.28 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding in northeastern Lehigh County and affected Washington and Heidelberg Townships the most where Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 to 4 inches. Measured event precipitation totals included 2.09 inches in Slatington and 1.48 inches in Schnecksville.

Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 1.5 to 2.5 inches across the extreme southern tier of Bucks County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant instability combined with modest lapse rates and abundant deep-layer moisture (PW) produced numerous thunderstorm clusters with locally heavy rain during the afternoon and evening hours across central PA. Although vertical wind shear profiles were not particularly strong, strong destabilization lead to robust updrafts which produced marginally severe hail and damaging winds/wet microbursts. Localized flash flooding occurred in Potter and McKean Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorm clusters produced heavy rain and localized flash flooding in and around the Shinglehouse area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of showers and thunderstorms withe heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of the Lehigh Valley, Poconos and Philadelphia northwest suburbs during the morning of the 1st. While additional showers and thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon, they were not as heavy. Creeks running higher than normal led to the death of a 13-year-old boy in Philadelphia that afternoon.  The 13-year-old boy and his 11-year-old brother jumped into the swollen Pennypack Creek near Roosevelt Boulevard (U.S. Route 1). They were swept by the swift current over a waterfall before an attempt could even be started to rescue them. One of the rescuers also had to be rescued. Doppler Radar storm total estimates ranged from 1.5 to 5.5 inches in sections of eastern Monroe, southern Lehigh and most of hardest hit Northampton County. The nearly stationary frontal boundary that has been responsible for heavy rain started to retrograde westward as a Bermuda High pressure system moved west from the Atlantic Ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused small stream and poor drainage flash flooding in eastern Monroe County in eastern sections of Smithfield and Middle Smithfield Townships. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 5 inches in the eastern parts of the townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first run of hot weather that combined high temperatures in the 90s and more oppressive humidity levels affected southeastern Pennsylvania and Berks County on July 6th and 7th and the Lehigh Valley on July 7th. High temperatures reached into the lower to mid 90s both days and combined with dew points of around 70 degrees produced afternoon heat index values of around 100 degrees.  High temperatures were about the same both days, but the 7th was slightly more humid. Morning low temperatures were in the 70s, with some upper 70s in and around Philadelphia.  A weak cold front dropped temperatures slightly on the 8th.||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone. A firefighter battling a blaze in the Frankford section of Philadelphia had to be treated for heat related problems on the 7th.||Highest temperatures included 95 degrees in Ambler (Montgomery County), 94 degrees in Philadelphia, Valley Creek (Chester County), Langhorne (Bucks County) and Martins Creek (Northampton County), 93 degrees in Reading (Berks County) and Aston (Delaware County) and 92 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Doylestown (Bucks County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along Farrandsville Road near Lock Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees down at the intersection of Standish Road and Kings Creek Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees and wires down at Spring Garden Ave and Borough St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported that flooding at the intersection of Route 66 and Italy Road has closed both roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms tied to a surface front  dropped several inches of rain over northwest Pennsylvania the morning of the 27th. Particularly hit hard were Jefferson and adjacent Clearfield counties, were roads were made impassable by over four to six inches of rainfall. The local EMA Director stated there were 49 water rescues and 50 people were placed in shelters. The hardest hit areas include Punxsutawney, Sykesville, Stump Creek, Big Run. and Reynoldsville. Preliminary damage reports have a total of 347 homes and 34 businesses damaged by flood waters. Of those, nine homes were destroyed and three businesses were a total loss. Damage is already estimated to be about $3 million but could be upwards of $5 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported water up to the seats in cars and a water rescues were ongoing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that moved across southeast Pennsylvania during the day on the 10th caused renewed flooding in the area. Event precipitation totals averaged between 1.5 and 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the 10th led to flash flooding of some roadways and gaged steams and creeks in Montgomery County. The Wissahickon Creek at Fort Washington was above its 9 foot flood stage from 748 p.m. EDT through 1141 p.m. EDT on the 10th, and it crested at 10.20 feet at 1000 p.m. EDT. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Schwenksville was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1138 a.m. EDT through 145 p.m. EDT on the 10th. It crested at 7.58 feet at 130 p.m. EDT. The river rose above its flood stage a second time from 1142 p.m. EDT on the 10th through 423 a.m. EDT on the 11th. The second crest was 7.79 feet at 230 a.m. EDT on the 11th. Event precipitation totals included 3.61 inches in North Wales, 3.33 inches in Hatfield, 2.99 inches in Kulpsville, 2.63 inches in Blue Bell, 2.45 inches in Harleysville, and 2.36 inches in Collegeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage. Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a 60 mph wind gust, measured at the Philadelphia International Airport at 141 a.m. EDT.  Radar imagery showed this thunderstorm wind gust to be the result of a wet microburst. The airport weather observer confirmed the occurrence of the wind gust. Elsewhere in the city, a downed tree forced the suspension of SEPTA commuter rail service between Center City and Thorndale in Chester County.  Service was restored later that day, but 30 minute delays along the line continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of showers and thunderstorms withe heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of the Lehigh Valley, Poconos and Philadelphia northwest suburbs during the morning of the 1st. While additional showers and thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon, they were not as heavy. Creeks running higher than normal led to the death of a 13-year-old boy in Philadelphia that afternoon.  The 13-year-old boy and his 11-year-old brother jumped into the swollen Pennypack Creek near Roosevelt Boulevard (U.S. Route 1). They were swept by the swift current over a waterfall before an attempt could even be started to rescue them. One of the rescuers also had to be rescued. Doppler Radar storm total estimates ranged from 1.5 to 5.5 inches in sections of eastern Monroe, southern Lehigh and most of hardest hit Northampton County. The nearly stationary frontal boundary that has been responsible for heavy rain started to retrograde westward as a Bermuda High pressure system moved west from the Atlantic Ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rains resulted in flash flooding in northeastern Northampton County.  Several home basements were flooded with two to five feet of water on Messinger Street in Bangor Borough as an underground creek rose and flooded. One family on Messenger Street was rescued. Two garages on the street were also damaged. Fuel oil leaked into the water. Flooding closed Messinger Road, Viaduct Road, South Main Street, and Rutt Road in the borough. No injuries were reported. Power was out to a number of homes for precautionary reasons also because water reached into the ground floor.  The flooding occurred because of an underground creek near the headwaters of Martins Creek.  Numerous streets were also flooded in Penn Argyl Borough.  Flooding waters also damaged a few homes in East Bangor Borough. The axis of heaviest rain extended from Penn Argyl and Washington Township northeastward through Upper Mount Bethel Township in Northampton County. Event precipitation totals included 3.54 inches in Nazareth and 2.07 inches in Bethlehem.  These municipalities were south of where the heaviest rain fell and Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 5 inches within the axis of heaviest rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected Eastern Pennsylvania from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only around 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 109 degrees in southeastern Pennsylvania and Berks County and 100 to 105 degrees elsewhere. The highest hourly heat index in Philadelphia was 107 degrees. There were two heat related deaths, both in Philadelphia. A 57-year-old male and a female died. While the 57-year-old male had an air conditioner in his home, it was not on when he was found. Four roof workers suffered heat exhaustion while working on the roof of the Chichester Middle School in Delaware County, three had to be hospitalized.| |To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone. Montgomery County issued a code red. Delaware County also provided fans to senior citizens.||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports, 96 degrees in Reading (Berks County), West Chester (Chester County) and Fork Township (Northampton County), 95 degrees in Doylestown and Quakertown in Bucks County and 94 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County). ||A stalling front brought an end to the excessive heat on the 21st and to 90 degree high temperatures by the 22nd.

Significant instability combined with modest lapse rates and abundant deep-layer moisture (PW) produced numerous thunderstorm clusters with locally heavy rain during the afternoon and evening hours across central PA. Although vertical wind shear profiles were not particularly strong, strong destabilization lead to robust updrafts which produced marginally severe hail and damaging winds/wet microbursts. Localized flash flooding occurred in Potter and McKean Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail (0.75 inch diameter) was reported near Shippensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm clusters across western Pennsylvania organized into bowing line segments as they moved east-southeast across the southern Alleghenies and Laurel Highlands during the late afternoon and early evening. The line of storms produced isolated wind damage and sub-severe hail. A more discrete thunderstorm cell just ahead of the main line of storms produced a brief, weak EF0 tornado in Somerset County. An area of locally heavy rain produced flash flooding in York County during the late evening, with areal flooding persisting into the early morning on 8/8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a few large trees and blew a dilapidated roof off of a barn near Jennerstown along Route 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first run of hot weather that combined high temperatures in the 90s and more oppressive humidity levels affected southeastern Pennsylvania and Berks County on July 6th and 7th and the Lehigh Valley on July 7th. High temperatures reached into the lower to mid 90s both days and combined with dew points of around 70 degrees produced afternoon heat index values of around 100 degrees.  High temperatures were about the same both days, but the 7th was slightly more humid. Morning low temperatures were in the 70s, with some upper 70s in and around Philadelphia.  A weak cold front dropped temperatures slightly on the 8th.||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone. A firefighter battling a blaze in the Frankford section of Philadelphia had to be treated for heat related problems on the 7th.||Highest temperatures included 95 degrees in Ambler (Montgomery County), 94 degrees in Philadelphia, Valley Creek (Chester County), Langhorne (Bucks County) and Martins Creek (Northampton County), 93 degrees in Reading (Berks County) and Aston (Delaware County) and 92 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Doylestown (Bucks County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in Philadelphia and Delaware Counties and also in parts of the Poconos and Lehigh Valley. Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of the Lehigh Valley, 6 to 8 inches in parts of the Poconos and around 8 inches in parts of the local Philadelphia area.  The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event. This event also helped establish back-to-back all-time monthly rainfall records in Philadelphia. The July 2013 precipitation total of 13.24 inches was a new monthly record for Philadelphia and came on the heels of a record breaking 10.56 inches falling in June. The current normal meteorological summer seasonal precipitation average for Philadelphia is 11.28 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding in southeastern Monroe County and affected Hamilton, Stroud, Smithfield and Middle Smithfield Townships as well as Stroudsburg and East Stroudsburg the hardest as Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 4 to 6 inches within parts of these municipalities. Measured event precipitation totals included 3.67 inches in East Stroudsburg, 2.92 inches in Middle Smithfield Township and 2.74 inches in Blakeslee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in Philadelphia and Delaware Counties and also in parts of the Poconos and Lehigh Valley. Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of the Lehigh Valley, 6 to 8 inches in parts of the Poconos and around 8 inches in parts of the local Philadelphia area.  The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event. This event also helped establish back-to-back all-time monthly rainfall records in Philadelphia. The July 2013 precipitation total of 13.24 inches was a new monthly record for Philadelphia and came on the heels of a record breaking 10.56 inches falling in June. The current normal meteorological summer seasonal precipitation average for Philadelphia is 11.28 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused urban and poor drainage flash flooding along all of the municipalities that border the Delaware River.  Event precipitation totals included 8.26 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.84 inches in Center City.  The precipitation gradient was tremendous over the county as only 0.80 inches fell in Swarthmore and 0.40 inches fell in Havertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage.||Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across eastern Pennsylvania. Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of poor drainage and roadway flooding, with some creeks and streams also overflowing their banks.||In addition, the combination of the heavy rain and higher than normal astronomical tides caused minor tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River during the early morning high tide cycles on the 23rd and lingered into the early morning high tide cycle on the 24th. In Philadelphia, the highest tide reached 8.76 feet above mean lower low water on the 23rd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell through the night of the 22nd and caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding to occur during the early morning of the 23rd. Wyomissing Creek was quickly out of its banks. Flooding was reported at the Cambridge Commons Apartments Complex, with water reported in the maintenance building. Flooding was also reported on Pennsylvania State Route 12 approaching Reading. Event precipitation totals included 6.70 inches in Kutztown, 4.75 inches in Boyertown, 4.21 inches in Bernville, 2.29 inches in Fleetwood and 2.23 inches in Oley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected Eastern Pennsylvania from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only around 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 109 degrees in southeastern Pennsylvania and Berks County and 100 to 105 degrees elsewhere. The highest hourly heat index in Philadelphia was 107 degrees. There were two heat related deaths, both in Philadelphia. A 57-year-old male and a female died. While the 57-year-old male had an air conditioner in his home, it was not on when he was found. Four roof workers suffered heat exhaustion while working on the roof of the Chichester Middle School in Delaware County, three had to be hospitalized.| |To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone. Montgomery County issued a code red. Delaware County also provided fans to senior citizens.||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports, 96 degrees in Reading (Berks County), West Chester (Chester County) and Fork Township (Northampton County), 95 degrees in Doylestown and Quakertown in Bucks County and 94 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County). ||A stalling front brought an end to the excessive heat on the 21st and to 90 degree high temperatures by the 22nd.

Severe thunderstorm clusters across western Pennsylvania organized into bowing line segments as they moved east-southeast across the southern Alleghenies and Laurel Highlands during the late afternoon and early evening. The line of storms produced isolated wind damage and sub-severe hail. A more discrete thunderstorm cell just ahead of the main line of storms produced a brief, weak EF0 tornado in Somerset County. An area of locally heavy rain produced flash flooding in York County during the late evening, with areal flooding persisting into the early morning on 8/8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm straight-line winds estimated near 80 mph knocked down the front and back walls and took the roof of a metal barn (the side walls remained standing). A steel wagon in the barn was turned on its side. Pieces of the roof were located several hundred yards to the east in a field along with several downed trees. The damage occurred in Lincoln Township along Keysertown and Bell Lane Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted looking south from Mile Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported a tree down blocking both westbound lanes of Route 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported flooding in Unity Square.

High temperatures reached into the lower to mid 90s both days and combined with dew points of around 70 degrees produced afternoon heat index values of around 100 degrees. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that moved across southeast Pennsylvania during the day on the 10th caused renewed flooding in the area. Event precipitation totals averaged between 1.5 and 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain that fell during the 10th led to flooding along gaged rivers and creeks in Philadelphia County. The Wissahickon Creek at the mouth in Philadelphia was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1135 p.m. EDT on the 10th through 425 a.m. EDT on the 11th. It crested at 5.60 feet at 200 a.m. EDT on the 11th. The tidal Delaware River at Philadelphia was above its 8.2 foot flood stage from 306 a.m. EDT through 413 a.m. EDT on the 11th, and it crested at 8.40 feet. Event precipitation totals included 2.20 inches in Rockledge and 1.37 inches in Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that moved across southeast Pennsylvania during the day on the 10th caused renewed flooding in the area. Event precipitation totals averaged between 1.5 and 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell during the 10th led to flash flooding along roadways and gaged creeks and streams in Delaware County. The Chester Creek at Chester had moderate flooding and was above its 8 foot flood stage from 636 p.m. EDT through 1049 p.m. EDT on the 10th, and it crested at 10.93 feet at 845 p.m. EDT. The Crum Creek at Newtown Square was above its 6 foot flood stage from 518 p.m. EDT through 1022 p.m. EDT on the 10th, and it crested at 9.34 feet at 700 p.m. EDT. Several roads were closed due to high water in Delaware County; including Westtown Road, Westtown-Thornton Road, and Creek Road. Event precipitation totals included 3.22 inches in Wayne and 1.37 inches in Chester Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in Philadelphia and Delaware Counties and also in parts of the Poconos and Lehigh Valley. Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of the Lehigh Valley, 6 to 8 inches in parts of the Poconos and around 8 inches in parts of the local Philadelphia area.  The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event. This event also helped establish back-to-back all-time monthly rainfall records in Philadelphia. The July 2013 precipitation total of 13.24 inches was a new monthly record for Philadelphia and came on the heels of a record breaking 10.56 inches falling in June. The current normal meteorological summer seasonal precipitation average for Philadelphia is 11.28 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavier rain caused minor flooding along the Valley Creek on the evening of the 28th. The Valley Creek at Valley Forge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 925 p.m. EDT through 956 p.m. EDT on the 28th. It crested at 7.11 feet at 945 p.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 2.32 inches in Kennett Square, 1.34 inches in Valley Forge and 1.19 inches in West Chester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance combined with  a hot and humid air mass to produce strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the second half of the afternoon and early evening on the 24th. In addition to locally damaging winds, lightning strikes caused one injury and damaged several homes. There were numerous reports of hail with the thunderstorms as the freezing level aloft was relatively low in height above the ground. Most of the hail was small (half an inch or less in diameter).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike damaged a home in Swarthmore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, extending from the Pennsylvania/Maryland border eastward across southern New Jersey, and a series of upper level disturbances resulted in numerous slow-moving thunderstorms from the afternoon of the 22nd through the early morning of the 23rd. These thunderstorms produced torrential rain and some wind damage.||Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 7 inches across eastern Pennsylvania. Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of poor drainage and roadway flooding, with some creeks and streams also overflowing their banks.||In addition, the combination of the heavy rain and higher than normal astronomical tides caused minor tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River during the early morning high tide cycles on the 23rd and lingered into the early morning high tide cycle on the 24th. In Philadelphia, the highest tide reached 8.76 feet above mean lower low water on the 23rd. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell through the night of the 22nd and caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding to occur during the early morning of the 23rd. Several roads around Schwenksville were reported to be flooded out. Cars were submerged within flood waters in Pottstown. No serious injuries were reported. Event precipitation totals included 3.32 inches in Gilbertsville, 2.90 inches in Blue Bell, 2.69 inches in North Wales, 2.57 inches in Kulpsville, 2.37 inches in Hatfield and 2.33 inches in Pottstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave and convective low dropping southeast from lower Michigan produced heavy showers and thunderstorms over Somerset County. The upper-air disturbance was embedded within an anomalously moist northwest flow and tapped into a plume of monsoonal moisture that wrapped northeast from the Desert Southwest around the northern periphery of an expansive southern Plains upper ridge. While much of Somerset County received 1 inch of rain during the course of the day, two concentrated areas of locally heavy rain (2-4 inches) occurred in the northwest and southwest corners of the county. The intense rain rates combined with wet antecedent conditions produced flash flooding which eventually transitioned into areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following a period of heavy rain and flash flooding earlier in the morning, a second area of heavy rain in Jenner Township and Boswell Borough lead to additional flash flooding and exacerbated ongoing inundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most oppressive hot spell of the summer season affected Eastern Pennsylvania from July 15th through the 20th.  Widespread high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s and the most oppressive days (combination of heat and humidity) occurred on the 18th and 19th. Morning lows those days were only around 80 degrees in highly urbanized areas. Afternoon heat indices reached 105 to 109 degrees in southeastern Pennsylvania and Berks County and 100 to 105 degrees elsewhere. The highest hourly heat index in Philadelphia was 107 degrees. There were two heat related deaths, both in Philadelphia. A 57-year-old male and a female died. While the 57-year-old male had an air conditioner in his home, it was not on when he was found. Four roof workers suffered heat exhaustion while working on the roof of the Chichester Middle School in Delaware County, three had to be hospitalized.| |To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone. Montgomery County issued a code red. Delaware County also provided fans to senior citizens.||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports, 96 degrees in Reading (Berks County), West Chester (Chester County) and Fork Township (Northampton County), 95 degrees in Doylestown and Quakertown in Bucks County and 94 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County). ||A stalling front brought an end to the excessive heat on the 21st and to 90 degree high temperatures by the 22nd.

Thunderstorms developed during the late morning over western Pennsylvania and intensified into a well-defined squall line /QLCS/ with bowing segments as it moved through central Pennsylvania during the afternoon. The line of storms produced corridors of severe straight-line winds which prompted numerous reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires along Spruce Street in Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported a trees and wires down in Springfield Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported numerous trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that moved across southeast Pennsylvania during the day on the 10th caused renewed flooding in the area. Event precipitation totals averaged between 1.5 and 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell during the 10th led to flashing flooding of some roadways and gaged creeks and streams in Bucks County. The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1123 p.m. EDT on the 10th through 625 a.m. EDT on the 11th. It crested at 9.60 feet at 145 a.m. EDT on the 11th. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Dublin was above its 5 foot flood stage from 802 p.m. EDT through 845 p.m. EDT on the 10th. It crested at 5.18 feet at 815 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 2.21 inches in Langhorne, 1.99 inches in Penndel, 1.51 inches in Pennsburg, and 1.33 inches in Quakertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning low end derecho moved through the region on the 13th which caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across southeastern Pennsylvania. The derecho moved off the New Jersey coast by late morning leaving a boundary across parts of the region. Later in the day, a strong low pressure system brought a cold front through the region, which sparked off more strong to severe thunderstorms across the area.||In addition to wind, heavy rain was reported with these storms. In general, 1 to 3 inches of rain fell across the state with the heaviest amounts reported across the Lehigh Valley. Rainfall amounts included 1.71 inches in Hellertown (Northampton County), 1.69 inches in South Allentown (Lehigh County), 1.61 inches in Upper Macungie Township and Macungie (Lehigh County), 1.56 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 1.23 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 1.20 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.10 inches in Landenburg (Chester County) and 1.02 inches in Alburtis (Berks County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees reported down, which also brought power lines down in East Marlborough Township.

Deep standing water occurred on Interstate 95. The heavy rain flooded sections of the 8th and Market PATCO Commuter rail line stop. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first run of hot weather that combined high temperatures in the 90s and more oppressive humidity levels affected southeastern Pennsylvania and Berks County on July 6th and 7th and the Lehigh Valley on July 7th. High temperatures reached into the lower to mid 90s both days and combined with dew points of around 70 degrees produced afternoon heat index values of around 100 degrees.  High temperatures were about the same both days, but the 7th was slightly more humid. Morning low temperatures were in the 70s, with some upper 70s in and around Philadelphia.  A weak cold front dropped temperatures slightly on the 8th.||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone. A firefighter battling a blaze in the Frankford section of Philadelphia had to be treated for heat related problems on the 7th.||Highest temperatures included 95 degrees in Ambler (Montgomery County), 94 degrees in Philadelphia, Valley Creek (Chester County), Langhorne (Bucks County) and Martins Creek (Northampton County), 93 degrees in Reading (Berks County) and Aston (Delaware County) and 92 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Doylestown (Bucks County).

A nearly stationary thunderstorm over northern Columbia County produced heavy rain and localized flash flooding during the early morning hours on the 2nd. The flooding was confined to the Sugarloaf Township/Benton area. Later that day, intense rainfall produced flash flooding in Northern Cambria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense heavy rainfall between 4-5 inches in less than an hour produced flash flooding in Northern Cambria. The bulk of the flooding was confined in Northern Cambria Borough and Susquehanna and Barr Townships. Deveaux Street and Route 219 North ��� both state routes - were closed temporarily due to flooding. Mosscreek Road in Barr Township received considerable debris and mud collection. The Northern Cambria Middle School reported significant flood damage and was closed for the week during the cleanup.  Numerous flooded basements were also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two houses were reported to be struck by lightning in the Broomall section of Marple Township. A house at the Women's Center at Swarthmore College was also struck by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that developed along a cold frontal boundary brought bands of showers and thunderstorms with heavy downpours to Delaware and Chester Counties on the 12th. This caused flooding. Event precipitation totals reached between 2.0 and 4.5 inches across central Delaware County and southern Chester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller streams and creeks in and around Upper Providence Township. Water rescues occurred within the township, but no serious injuries were reported. Event precipitation totals included 4.15 inches in Media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a deep southerly flow of very moist air caused slow moving and in some instances back building thunderstorms to occur during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. This caused an all-time record breaking rainfall in Philadelphia as well as flash flooding in Philadelphia and Delaware Counties and also in parts of the Poconos and Lehigh Valley. Doppler Radar storm total estimates and or actual measurements reached around 4 inches in parts of the Lehigh Valley, 6 to 8 inches in parts of the Poconos and around 8 inches in parts of the local Philadelphia area.  The daily rainfall measurement of 8.02 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport was not only the all-time calendar day record but was estimated to be a one in thousand year event. This event also helped establish back-to-back all-time monthly rainfall records in Philadelphia. The July 2013 precipitation total of 13.24 inches was a new monthly record for Philadelphia and came on the heels of a record breaking 10.56 inches falling in June. The current normal meteorological summer seasonal precipitation average for Philadelphia is 11.28 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused urban and poor drainage flash flooding in Philadelphia. Multiple ramps of Interstate 95 around Broad Street were closed. A 21-year-old woman drowned after she accidentally drove into the Schuylkill River near Boathouse Road in Philadelphia. A 31-year-old male passenger managed to swim to safety. The river was running higher than average, but was not flooding at the time of the drowning. SEPTA Regional rail lines reported flooding on tracks within Philadelphia. The flooding rains caused portions of Terminal A at Philadelphia International Airport to lose power. Departure and arrival delays reached 90 minutes and seventeen flights were cancelled. Event precipitation totals included 8.26 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.84 inches in Center City.  The precipitation gradient was tremendous over Philadelphia as only 0.64 inches fell at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport.||A total of 8.02 inches of rain fell at the Philadelphia International Airport on the 28th. The amount not only established a new record daily maximum rainfall for that date, but was also the most rainfall on record for any calendar day in Philadelphia. The previous record was set on September 16, 1999 during Tropical Storm Floyd and was 6.63 inches.  This torrential downpour also helped establish a new July monthly rainfall record of 13.24 inches in Philadelphia. The previous record was 10.42 inches set in 1994.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance combined with  a hot and humid air mass to produce strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the second half of the afternoon and early evening on the 24th. In addition to locally damaging winds, lightning strikes caused one injury and damaged several homes. There were numerous reports of hail with the thunderstorms as the freezing level aloft was relatively low in height above the ground. Most of the hail was small (half an inch or less in diameter).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees, poles  and wires across a couple of roads in Nazareth. Trees were reported knocked down on Graceland Avenue and a downed tree damaged a vehicle (no injuries were reported) on Myers Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system acted on a moist and unstable airmasss to bring severe thunderstorms to northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several un-named creeks were out of their banks. Arnes Road bridge was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and lee side trof helped trigger strong to locally severe thunderstorms across extreme southeastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon on the 17th. The thunderstorms also produced some small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in London Grove Township. The tree damage was concentrated around the Chatham area near the intersection of Pennsylvania State Route 41 and U.S. Route 1.  Several hundred homes and businesses in the township lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported Airport Road closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave and convective low dropping southeast from lower Michigan produced heavy showers and thunderstorms over Somerset County. The upper-air disturbance was embedded within an anomalously moist northwest flow and tapped into a plume of monsoonal moisture that wrapped northeast from the Desert Southwest around the northern periphery of an expansive southern Plains upper ridge. While much of Somerset County received 1 inch of rain during the course of the day, two concentrated areas of locally heavy rain (2-4 inches) occurred in the northwest and southwest corners of the county. The intense rain rates combined with wet antecedent conditions produced flash flooding which eventually transitioned into areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread areal flooding persisted through the evening hours in the Boswell area. Small streams and creeks returned to their banks as flood waters receded into the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave and convective low dropping southeast from lower Michigan produced heavy showers and thunderstorms over Somerset County. The upper-air disturbance was embedded within an anomalously moist northwest flow and tapped into a plume of monsoonal moisture that wrapped northeast from the Desert Southwest around the northern periphery of an expansive southern Plains upper ridge. While much of Somerset County received 1 inch of rain during the course of the day, two concentrated areas of locally heavy rain (2-4 inches) occurred in the northwest and southwest corners of the county. The intense rain rates combined with wet antecedent conditions produced flash flooding which eventually transitioned into areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized heavy rainfall of 3-4 inches in about 3 hours caused flash flooding in Jenner Township and Boswell Borough. The North Star School District was closed due to the flooding. Route 601 was closed in Jenners and several homes had flooded basements. One vehicle got stranded in moving water along Route 601 in the flood waters. Large boulders washed out onto the roadway on US 30 (Lincoln Highway) in the village of Jenners. Several roads were closed in Boswell Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding in Berks County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached two to three inches in sections of the county.   Extremely wet antecedent conditions made it easier for flash flooding to occur. In fact, it was the wettest June on record in Philadelphia (10.56 inches)  and the 6th wettest June on record in Allentown (6.83 inches). On a county weighted average, June monthly precipitation ranged from 6.9 inches in Berks County to 10.4 inches in Chester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of small creeks as well as roadway flooding in and around Bethel Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system acted on a moist and unstable airmasss to bring severe thunderstorms to northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Equinunk Creek was over its banks and flooding Equinunk Creek Road in several places. Two bridges were underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system acted on a moist and unstable airmasss to bring severe thunderstorms to northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Winterdale Road were washed out by flash flooding. Shoulders and guard rails of the road were gone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A retrograding upper level low continued to bring the threat for showers and thunderstorms on June 29th. However, with meager instability and shear, most storms were able to remain below severe levels, and only a few strong to severe wind gusts and toppled trees were reported. Despite this, a relatively stationary boundary did leave enough focus for some training thunderstorms that produced flooding over portions of Jefferson and Armstrong counties in Pennsylvania; two counties that had already experienced several days of heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported flooding on River Road, East Branch Road, and Coolspring Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall across western Pennsylvania and the northern West Virginia Panhandle. Flooding of roadways and areas of poor drainage was reported.  Significant flash flooding was reported across portions of Fayette county in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coveland Creek was first reported out of its banks and debris was moving a bridge. From South Connellsville to Dunbar Borough, Dunbar Township and North Union a state of emergency was declared as flash flooding washed out roads, and damaged yards and homes across these areas. An elderly woman was evacuated from her home in North Union, where from 120 to 130 homes had water damage, and at least 14 homes were flooded by Dunbar Creek. Damage to township roads in North Union was estimated by officials to be one million dollars alone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding that occurred during the predawn hours transitioned into areal flooding and persisted through the late morning. Several small streams and creeks exceeded bankful levels and flooded nearby roads and low-lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm clusters across western Pennsylvania organized into bowing line segments as they moved east-southeast across the southern Alleghenies and Laurel Highlands during the late afternoon and early evening. The line of storms produced isolated wind damage and sub-severe hail. A more discrete thunderstorm cell just ahead of the main line of storms produced a brief, weak EF0 tornado in Somerset County. An area of locally heavy rain produced flash flooding in York County during the late evening, with areal flooding persisting into the early morning on 8/8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter inch hail (1 inch diameter) was reported near Amberson Valley.

In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees down on Langeloth Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous roads closed in New castle due to flash flooding with some cars stranded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported numerous trees and power lines down in and around Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall across western Pennsylvania and the northern West Virginia Panhandle. Flooding of roadways and areas of poor drainage was reported.  Significant flash flooding was reported across portions of Fayette county in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gage from COE showed 3.50 inches of rain during flash flood event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across western Pennsylvania in a hot and humid air mass with weak upper level winds. A few storms became severe, and heavy rainfall produced flash flooding and areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported Multiple trees down on Walnut Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

Severe thunderstorm clusters across western Pennsylvania organized into bowing line segments as they moved east-southeast across the southern Alleghenies and Laurel Highlands during the late afternoon and early evening. The line of storms produced isolated wind damage and sub-severe hail. A more discrete thunderstorm cell just ahead of the main line of storms produced a brief, weak EF0 tornado in Somerset County. An area of locally heavy rain produced flash flooding in York County during the late evening, with areal flooding persisting into the early morning on 8/8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along Route 255 southwest of Penfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in southern sections of Chester and Delaware Counties and across central Carbon County during the late afternoon and early evening on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding in southern Delaware County. The Garnet Valley area of Bethel Township. Thunderstorms dropped 2.25 inches of rain in the area within 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in southern sections of Chester and Delaware Counties and across central Carbon County during the late afternoon and early evening on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding along the southern tier of Chester County.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates averaged 2 to 3 inches in this area. The White Clay Creek at Strickersville was above its 9.5 foot flood stage from 757 p.m. EDT through 910 p.m. EDT on the 28th. It crested at 9.75 feet at 845 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 2.47 inches in Landenburg.

Doppler Radar storm total estimates averaged 2 to 3 inches in this area.  Event precipitation totals included 1.75 inches in Coaldale and 1.58 inches in Jim Thorpe.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flash flooding, with a house surrounded by water and a water rescue taking place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage in McKean County after midnight. A weak shortwave moved eastward across eastern KY during the predawn hours into the early morning, preceded by a field of large-scale lift and deep moisture transport focused into the lower Susquehanna Valley. Although storm motions were relatively quick, areas of training combined with 2+ precipitable water values, intense precipitation rates and seasonably high stream flows lead to flash flooding and road closures. In addition, low instability coupled with modest low-level shear produced a few supercell storm-structures and isolated damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed runoff from heavy rain and flash flooding from early in the morning continued areal flooding problems and road closures through the afternoon. A water rescue was conducted in Warwick Township at Cocalico/Rothsville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding in Berks County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached two to three inches in sections of the county.   Extremely wet antecedent conditions made it easier for flash flooding to occur. In fact, it was the wettest June on record in Philadelphia (10.56 inches)  and the 6th wettest June on record in Allentown (6.83 inches). On a county weighted average, June monthly precipitation ranged from 6.9 inches in Berks County to 10.4 inches in Chester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of small creeks as well as roadway flooding in Muhlenberg and Ruscombmanor Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning low end derecho moved through the region on the 13th which caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across southeastern Pennsylvania. The derecho moved off the New Jersey coast by late morning leaving a boundary across parts of the region. Later in the day, a strong low pressure system brought a cold front through the region, which sparked off more strong to severe thunderstorms across the area.||In addition to wind, heavy rain was reported with these storms. In general, 1 to 3 inches of rain fell across the state with the heaviest amounts reported across the Lehigh Valley. Rainfall amounts included 1.71 inches in Hellertown (Northampton County), 1.69 inches in South Allentown (Lehigh County), 1.61 inches in Upper Macungie Township and Macungie (Lehigh County), 1.56 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 1.23 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 1.20 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.10 inches in Landenburg (Chester County) and 1.02 inches in Alburtis (Berks County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was struck by lightning on Bridal Court in New Garden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea moving up the Eastern Seaboard, from the Southeast States to the Mid Atlantic and Northeast Coasts, led to waves of heavy rain and thunderstorms from the early morning hours through the late evening on the 7th. The initial burst of heavy precipitation arrived during the early morning on the 7th as moisture surged into the Mid Atlantic Region ahead of Tropical Storm Andrea, with the center of its low pressure located near the coast of Georgia. Through the course of the day, the cyclone became post-tropical as it accelerated northeastward along the East Coast of the United States, delivering another round of heavy rain with some strong thunderstorms to the Mid Atlantic States during the late afternoon and evening of the 7th. ||Doppler radar storm total estimates ranged from around 2 to 4 inches of rain for the event across eastern Pennsylvania.  Waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of flooding, including along Chester Creek in Delaware County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flooding caused about 50 families in Chester to evacuate along the Chester Creek. Flooding occurred along a couple gaged streams in Delaware County.  The Crum Creek at Newtown Square was above its 6 foot flood stage from 440 p.m. EDT through 933 p.m. EDT on the 7th, and it crested at 6.68 feet at 545 p.m. EDT.  Heavy rain also led to flooding along the Chester Creek at Chester, and fifty families in the area were evacuated.  The Chester Creek at Chester was above its 8 foot flood stage from 423 p.m. EDT through 1110 p.m. EDT on the 7th, and it crested at 9.80 feet at 615 p.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 4.13 inches in Lansdowne, 4.01 inches in Drexel Hill, 3.71 inches in Aston Township, 3.22 inches in Chester Township, and 3.16 inches in Wayne.

A retrograding upper level low continued to bring the threat for showers and thunderstorms on June 29th. However, with meager instability and shear, most storms were able to remain below severe levels, and only a few strong to severe wind gusts and toppled trees were reported. Despite this, a relatively stationary boundary did leave enough focus for some training thunderstorms that produced flooding over portions of Jefferson and Armstrong counties in Pennsylvania; two counties that had already experienced several days of heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees down along Bradys Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm clusters across western Pennsylvania organized into bowing line segments as they moved east-southeast across the southern Alleghenies and Laurel Highlands during the late afternoon and early evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey conducted in Quemahoning Township in Somerset County confirmed a brief, weak EF0 tornado occurred a few miles east of Ralphton near Horner Church Road. One home sustained minor damage and one barn roof was partially torn off. Over 2 dozen trees were knocked down or damaged. The estimated maximum wind speed was between 70-80 mph. The maximum path width was 75 yards with a path length of about 0.25 mile. There were no injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slowly moving cold front with a wave of low pressure along it and wet antecedent conditions helped develop a large area of light to moderate rain with embedded thunderstorms accompanied by heavy rain in extreme southeast Pennsylvania primarily during the afternoon of the 18th. This led to urban and poor drainage flooding and also flash flooding near and along some of the smaller creeks. Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 3 inches and this was mainly confined to Chester and Delaware Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Valley Creek at Valley Forge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 201 p.m. EDT through 417 p.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 7.49 feet at 345 p.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 2.70 inches in East Nantmeal Township and 1.78 inches in Honey Brook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea moving up the Eastern Seaboard, from the Southeast States to the Mid Atlantic and Northeast Coasts, led to waves of heavy rain and thunderstorms from the early morning hours through the late evening on the 7th. The initial burst of heavy precipitation arrived during the early morning on the 7th as moisture surged into the Mid Atlantic Region ahead of Tropical Storm Andrea, with the center of its low pressure located near the coast of Georgia. Through the course of the day, the cyclone became post-tropical as it accelerated northeastward along the East Coast of the United States, delivering another round of heavy rain with some strong thunderstorms to the Mid Atlantic States during the late afternoon and evening of the 7th. ||Doppler radar storm total estimates ranged from around 2 to 4 inches of rain for the event across eastern Pennsylvania.  Waves of heavy precipitation resulted in areas of flooding, including along Chester Creek in Delaware County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along a couple of gaged streams in Chester County.  The Valley Creek at Valley Forge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 439 p.m. EDT through 654 p.m. EDT on the 7th, and it crested at 7.38 feet at 515 p.m. EDT.  The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 757 p.m. EDT through 1020 p.m. EDT on the 7th, and it crested at 7.13 feet at 900 p.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 4.24 inches in Unionville, 4.18 inches in Landenberg and Chatham, 3.99 inches in West Chester and Franklin, 3.98 inches in New London, and 3.92 inches in Spring City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slowly moving cold front with a wave of low pressure along it and wet antecedent conditions helped develop a large area of light to moderate rain with embedded thunderstorms accompanied by heavy rain in extreme southeast Pennsylvania primarily during the afternoon of the 18th. This led to urban and poor drainage flooding and also flash flooding near and along some of the smaller creeks. Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 3 inches and this was mainly confined to Chester and Delaware Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavier rain that fell during the afternoon of the 18th caused minor flooding along the Crum Creek. The Crum Creek at Newtown Square was above its 6 foot flood stage from 521 p.m. EDT through 611 p.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 6.31 feet at 545 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 2.01 inches in Norwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm clusters across western Pennsylvania organized into bowing line segments as they moved east-southeast across the southern Alleghenies and Laurel Highlands during the late afternoon and early evening. The line of storms produced isolated wind damage and sub-severe hail. A more discrete thunderstorm cell just ahead of the main line of storms produced a brief, weak EF0 tornado in Somerset County. An area of locally heavy rain produced flash flooding in York County during the late evening, with areal flooding persisting into the early morning on 8/8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail (1 inch diameter) was reported near Amberson Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A retrograding upper level low continued to bring the threat for showers and thunderstorms on June 29th. However, with meager instability and shear, most storms were able to remain below severe levels, and only a few strong to severe wind gusts and toppled trees were reported. Despite this, a relatively stationary boundary did leave enough focus for some training thunderstorms that produced flooding over portions of Jefferson and Armstrong counties in Pennsylvania; two counties that had already experienced several days of heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported one tree down on Canal Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary situated over interstate 80 became the focus for thunderstorm development as several upper level disturbances rounded a flattening ridge over the plains. These storms eventually congealed into several lines, one near the western Pennsylvania/Ohio border and another further back into Ohio southern lower Michigan, that moved southeastward through the evening of the 25th and early on the 26th. In addition to widespread reports of wind damage and hail, training thunderstorms dumped two to four inches of rain and caused areas of flooding across east central Ohio and adjacent areas in western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees down.

The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue in Philadelphia was above its 7 foot flood stage from 405 p.m. EDT through 421 p.m. EDT on the 7th, and it crested at 7.28 feet at 415 p.m. EDT. It was above its 7 foot flood stage from 645 p.m. EDT on the 7th through 210 a.m. EDT on the 8th, and it crested at 8.21 feet at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 8th.
