EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-topped squall line accompanied a sharp cold front across central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening. The squall line produced localized severe wind gusts, resulting in sporadic wind damage across south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down utility wires in Johnstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-topped squall line accompanied a sharp cold front across central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening. The squall line produced localized severe wind gusts, resulting in sporadic wind damage across south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along SR 22 in Porter Township near Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-topped squall line accompanied a sharp cold front across central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening. The squall line produced localized severe wind gusts, resulting in sporadic wind damage across south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along Cold Springs Road in Huntingdon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A belt of seasonably strong lower-mid tropospheric flow in the Ohio Valley on the eastern periphery of a large-scale upper trough impinged on a moist and unstable environment over central Pennsylvania and resulted in severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Multicell thunderstorm clusters organized into mini bowing line segments which produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near the Shiloh Road exit along I-99 near the Benner Pike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A belt of seasonably strong lower-mid tropospheric flow in the Ohio Valley on the eastern periphery of a large-scale upper trough impinged on a moist and unstable environment over central Pennsylvania and resulted in severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Multicell thunderstorm clusters organized into mini bowing line segments which produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires around Lock Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of wraparound moisture from the departing nor'easter and a new upper low disturbance brought accumulating snow to extreme Eastern Pennsylvania overnight on the 7th into the afternoon of the 8th. Snowfall averaged 2 to 4 inches in counties along the Delaware River from Philadelphia north with lesser amounts in counties farther to the west.||Intermittent precipitation started again on the 7th as rain with some wet snow mixed in over the Poconos. As the precipitation intensity increased, the rain changed to snow in the Poconos during the late afternoon on the 7th, but took most of the overnight for it to occur in the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania. The snow fell at its heaviest during the morning commute on the 8th. While the snow persisted into the afternoon across far Eastern Pennsylvania on the 8th (it ended during the morning farther to the west), as intensity lessened and indirect insolation from the March sun prevented much snow from additionally accumulating from the late morning onward.  In fact, the snow changed to rain before ending that afternoon in the local Philadelphia area. ||Representative snowfall included 3.5 inches in Bensalem (Bucks County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Furlong (Bucks County) and Hellertown (Northampton County), 2.8 inches in Horsham (Montgomery County) and Somerton (Philadelphia), 2.0 inches in Penndel (Bucks County) and Rockledge (Philadelphia), 1.4 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 1.1 inches in Wayne (Delaware County), 0.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 0.2 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

A wintry mix of precipitation affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania except for Philadelphia and its immediate suburbs from the afternoon of the 18th into the morning of the 19th. In Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, snowfall averaged two to six inches and ice accretions average one to three tenths of an inch, highest in the southern Poconos. In the Philadelphia northern suburbs snowfall averaged around an inch and little if any snow accumulated toward Philadelphia. Untreated roadways were hazardous. It was snowing heavily during the evening commute on the 18th.||Precipitation started as snow during the mid to late afternoon on the 18th and fell at its heaviest during that evening commute. In the Philadelphia northern suburbs and Berks County, the snow changed to rain by the middle of that evening.  In the Lehigh Valley and Poconos, the snow changed to sleet and then to freezing rain that evening. In the Lehigh Valley, the freezing rain changed to plain rain early on the 19th, but a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain persisted in the Poconos until precipitation ended on the morning of the 19th. Travel was particularly difficult in the Poconos and accidents were reported in Monroe County. Traffic was reported as difficult along Pennsylvania State Routes 115 and 715 and U.S. Route 209.||Representative snowfall included 6.0 inches in Summit Hill and Lake Harmony in Carbon County, 5.8 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Bowmanstown (Carbon County) and New Tripoli and Schnecksville in Lehigh County, 3.5 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 3.2 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County) and Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 3.0 inches in Bernville (Berks County) and Danielsville (Northamptobn County), 2.7 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 2.5 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 2.3 inches in Reading (Berks County), 1.9 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.5 inches in Glendon (Northampton County), 1.3 inches in Elverson (Chester County), 1.2 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and 1.0 inch in Doylestown (Bucks County) and Harleysville (Montgomery County).  Representative ice accumulations included 0.3 of an inch in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 0.25 inches in Stormville (Monroe County) and 0.1 inches in Summit Hill (Carbon County). ||The wintry weather was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Southern Plains on the morning of the 17th eastnortheast and reached the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 18th and the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 18th. A secondary low pressure system formed on the system's warm front along the Carolina coast overnight on the 18th. This low slowly became the primary low pressure system as it moved northeast and passed east of the New Jersey coast on the morning of the 19th. A high pressure system paralleled the track of the low as it moved from the Great Lakes on the morning of the 17th and into New England on the morning of the 18th. It kept initially dry and then sufficiently cold air in place near the surface to perpetuate the wintry precipitation, especially in the Poconos.

A nor'easter that moved east of the state on the 25th dropped 2 to 5 inches of snow in the Philadelphia western suburbs and Berks County and 1 to 3 inches of snow elsewhere across Eastern Pennsylvania including Philadelphia. Most of the snow fell during the day and had difficulty accumulating when the intensity decreased. Nevertheless, the heaviest snow fell throughout the morning commute in the southeast part of the state and caused slushy roadways and traffic accidents. In Chester County, multiple accidents were reported along U.S. Route 202. In Delaware County, a tractor-trailer accident on Interstate 95 snarled the morning commute. In addition, a few school districts cancelled classes or had early dismissals and isolated power outages also occurred in the Philadelphia suburbs. West Chester University (Chester County) cancelled evening classes. The snow canceled some flights and caused delays exceeding one hour at the Philadelphia International Airport. The snow started between 6 a.m. and 8 a.m. EDT on the 25th and fell at its heaviest nearly instantly in the southeast part of the state and through the morning commute. The snow was generally lighter in intensity in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley. While snow continued during the late morning into the afternoon, the intensity was less and  it did not accumulate as efficiently. As precipitation intensity lessened even more, the snow changed to light rain during the late afternoon from the Lehigh Valley southward. Precipitation did not change to rain in the Poconos.  All of the precipitation ended during the evening of the 25th. ||Representative snowfall included 4.5 inches in Glenmoore and East Nantmeal (Chester County), 4.0 inches in Chester Springs (Chester County), 3.9 inches in Mohnton (Berks County) and Nottingham (Chester County), 3.8 inches in Royersford (Montgomery County), 3.5 inches in Glen Mills (Delaware County), 3.3 inches in Kennett Square (Chester County), 3.2 inches in Wayne (Delaware County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 3.1 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 3.0 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County) and Exton (Chester County), 2.8 inches in Reading (Berks County), Brookhaven (Delaware County), Limerick (Montgomery County) and Spring City (Chester County), 2.5 inches in Summit Hill (Carbon County), Penndel (Bucks County) and Bernville (Berks County), 2.3 inches in Souderton (Montgomery County), 2.0 inches in Doylestown (Bucks County), Forks Township (Northampton County), Willow Grove (Montgomery County), Easton (Northampton County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 1.8 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 1.6 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County), 1.5 inches in West Grove (Chester County) and Harleysville (Montgomery County), 1.4 inches in Quakertown (Bucks County), 1.2 inches in Rockledge (Philadelphia County), 1.1 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 1.0 inch in Bowmanstown (Carbon County), Palmerton (Carbon County) and Bethlehem (Northampton County) and 0.8 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.| |The low pressure system responsible for the late season snow moved from Alabama on the morning of the 24th northeast and passed just east of Norfolk, Virginia at 8 a.m. EDT on the 25th. It proceeded to move northeast and by 8 p.m. EDT on the 25th it was about 175 miles east of the New Jersey coast.  The combination of the relatively offshore storm track coupled with most of the precipitation falling during the day and in late March (relatively strong indirect sunlight) prevented heavier snow from occurring or accumulating.

A low-topped squall line accompanied a sharp cold front across central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening. The squall line produced localized severe wind gusts, resulting in sporadic wind damage across south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires on Main Street in Mercersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-topped squall line accompanied a sharp cold front across central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening. The squall line produced localized severe wind gusts, resulting in sporadic wind damage across south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Greencastle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms intensified over northeast West Virginia and tracked northeastward across central Pennsylvania during the mid/late afternoon hours. Strong wind fields aloft and moderate instability promoted numerous reports of damaging winds across a large portion of the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down several trees along US 22. The downed trees briefly closed both east/west bound lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A belt of seasonably strong lower-mid tropospheric flow in the Ohio Valley on the eastern periphery of a large-scale upper trough impinged on a moist and unstable environment over central Pennsylvania and resulted in severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Multicell thunderstorm clusters organized into mini bowing line segments which produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Gamble Township near Calvert.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees across West Juniata Parkway near Newport.

A low pressure system acted on a moist and unstable airmasss to bring severe thunderstorms to northeast Pennsylvania.
A low pressure system acted on a moist and unstable airmasss to bring severe thunderstorms to northeast Pennsylvania.

A belt of seasonably strong lower-mid tropospheric flow in the Ohio Valley on the eastern periphery of a large-scale upper trough impinged on a moist and unstable environment over central Pennsylvania and resulted in severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Multicell thunderstorm clusters organized into mini bowing line segments which produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was measured atop the Walker Building on the PSU campus.

Multicell thunderstorm clusters organized into mini bowing line segments which produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires in Sunbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front with waves of low pressure along it caused rain,  heavy at times,  to fall across Eastern Pennsylvania from around Midnight EDT on the 12th into that afternoon. Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to around 2.0 inches across the Poconos, Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the northern Philadelphia suburbs with lesser amounts toward Philadelphia itself. This rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as isolated creek flooding. The rain started between Midnight EDT and 2 a.m. EDT on the 12th, fell at its heaviest during the morning and into early afternoon and ended during the middle to late afternoon on the 12th as the slow moving cold front cleared the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Main Stem of the Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville was above its 4 foot flood stage from 256 p.m. EDT through 714 p.m. EDT on the12th. It crested at 4.56 feet at 6 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 1.74 inches in East Greenville and Gilbertsville,  1.44 inches in Pottstown and 1.35 inches in Green Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient (difference) between a high pressure system over the Gulf Coast States and a low pressure system over Eastern Canada produced strong gusty west to northwest winds over the higher terrain of Eastern Pennsylvania and in the local Philadelphia area during the afternoon of the 14th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and knocked down weak tree limbs and wires. Peak wind gusts included 47 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 44 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharpening upper air trough helped form a cold front and snow showers and flurries across Eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning of the 21st. The highest snowfall (up to 1.0 inch) occurred in the Philadelphia suburbs and caused slippery travel for overnight drivers. The snow showers began between Midnight EDT and 2 a.m. EDT on the 21st and ended by 4 a.m. EDT. Representative snowfall included 1.0 inch in Honey Brook (Chester County) and 0.5 inches in Hatfield (Montgomery County), East Nantmeal (Chester County) and Furlong and Doylestown in Bucks County.

Isolated supercells shifted east of the Lake Erie region and produced two tornadoes in Warren County during the late evening. Both tornadoes occurred in the northern half of the county and were relatively small/short-lived and weak. The first (EF0) tornado was embedded within a larger area of straight-line thunderstorm wind damage, while the second, more significant (EF1) tornado had a longer track and caused more damage. Official NWS storm surveys were conducted in conjunction with Warren County Emergency Management.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey team comprised of NWS meteorologists and Warren County Emergency Management officials determined a weak EF0 tornado occurred during the late evening of May 28th. The tornado first touched down in northwest Brokenstraw township on Murray Hill Road. The tornado touched down and lifted several times...finally lifting just east of Brown Hill Road in Sugar Grove Township. The estimated maximum winds were 80 mph along the sporadic path. The EF0 tornado moved northeast along a 2 3/4 mile path for about 4 minutes. The tornado was embedded within a larger area of straight-line winds. A total of about 400 trees were knocked down or uprooted. Four homes and a church sustained minor structural damage. Two outbuildings also sustained significant damage. There were no injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex (MCC) with vivid lightning barreled through south-central Pennsylvania from northwest to southeast during the early morning hours.   Thunderstorms organized into a bowed segment as they accelerated eastward, producing areas of wind damage across the southern portion the CWA. The damaging winds knocked down numerous trees and wires causing several thousands of customers to lose power in various locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along Fords Corner Road in Jackson Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense nor'easter brought strong northeast winds to southeastern Pennsylvania on the 6th. Peak wind gusts reached 45 to 50 mph and downed weak trees, tree limbs and wires and caused scattered power outages.  A few thousand home and businesses lost power. The heavy rain and wind also caused flight delays and cancellations at the Philadelphia International Airport. There were 137 cancelled flights on the 6th and arrival flight delays reached three hours. There was also an empty trailer ban on the Walt Whitman and Commodore Barry Bridges in and near Philadelphia. ||Peak wind gusts included 49 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Doylestown (Bucks County),  47 mph in Wayne (Delaware County), 44 mph in West Chester (Chester County) and 40 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County).  ||The nor'easter low pressure system emerged from the southern Rockies on the 4th and moved into the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 5th, passed across the southern Appalachians during the evening of the 5th and reached northeastern North Carolina on the morning of the 6th. From there it slowly moved northeast and was off the Delmarva Peninsula on the afternoon of the 6th. It then drifted slowly offshore to the east that evening and that motion continued on the 7th and 8th. The low pressure system was not that intense overall (never deepened to less than 985 millibars near the coast), but a strong high pressure system that was located over southeastern Canada helped intensify the surface pressure gradient (difference) throughout this event.

A quick moving low pressure system dropped 1 to 3 inches of snow across Berks County, the Lehigh Valley, the Poconos and higher terrain locations in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs. While snowfall amounts were not heavy, it fell heavily briefly and caused a slushy accumulation on less traveled roadways, bridges and overpasses.  Accidents were also reported in Chester County on the Pennsylvania Turnpike west of Downingtown. Temperatures fell below freezing over the higher terrain overnight and untreated roadways were slippery. Snow began falling during the second half of the morning on the 16th, fell at its heaviest during the first half of the afternoon and ended close to 6 p.m. EDT on the 16th. The snow changed to rain in the local Philadelphia area during the afternoon of the 16th. Representative snowfall included 3.0 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County) and Albrightsville (Carbon County), 2.5 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 2.3 inches in West Rockhill Township (Bucks County),  2.2 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.0 inches in Hilltown Township (Bucks County) and Summit Hill (Carbon County), 1.6 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County), 1.5 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 1.1 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.0 inch in Elverson (Chester County) and Souderton (Montgomery County).||The snow was caused by a quick moving low pressure system that moved from the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 16th, reached West Virginia at 2 p.m. EDT on the 16th and moved off the lower Delmarva Peninsula on the evening of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense nor'easter brought strong northeast winds to southeastern Pennsylvania on the 6th. Peak wind gusts reached 45 to 50 mph and downed weak trees, tree limbs and wires and caused scattered power outages.  A few thousand home and businesses lost power. The heavy rain and wind also caused flight delays and cancellations at the Philadelphia International Airport. There were 137 cancelled flights on the 6th and arrival flight delays reached three hours. There was also an empty trailer ban on the Walt Whitman and Commodore Barry Bridges in and near Philadelphia. ||Peak wind gusts included 49 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Doylestown (Bucks County),  47 mph in Wayne (Delaware County), 44 mph in West Chester (Chester County) and 40 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County).  ||The nor'easter low pressure system emerged from the southern Rockies on the 4th and moved into the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 5th, passed across the southern Appalachians during the evening of the 5th and reached northeastern North Carolina on the morning of the 6th. From there it slowly moved northeast and was off the Delmarva Peninsula on the afternoon of the 6th. It then drifted slowly offshore to the east that evening and that motion continued on the 7th and 8th. The low pressure system was not that intense overall (never deepened to less than 985 millibars near the coast), but a strong high pressure system that was located over southeastern Canada helped intensify the surface pressure gradient (difference) throughout this event.

A cold front swept east across the upper Ohio Valley on March 20th. Cold west to northwest winds developed behind the front causing lake effect snow showers to develop. The snow showers were intermittent and finally ended during the afternoon hours of the 22nd. The snow was heavy at times, especially during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st when visibilities were less than a half mile at times. Winds gusted to more than 25 mph causing some blowing and drifting. Snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were reported across much of inland Erie County and northern Crawford County.  A few of the higher totals In Erie County included 18.0 inches south of North East, 17.3 inches at Franklin Center and 17.2 inches near Colt Station.  A peak total of 10.5 inches was reported near Meadville in Crawford County.  A few accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed on the morning of March 20th and finally ended during the afternoon hours of the 22nd. The snow was heavy at times, especially during the afternoon and evening hours of the 21st when visibilities were less than a half mile at times. Winds gusted to more than 20 mph causing some blowing and drifting.  A peak total of 10.5 inches was reported near Meadville in Crawford County. A few accidents were reported.

An area of low pressure moved north across western Ohio on March 18th. A warm front stretching east from the low also lifted north across the region. Rain developed along this front during the morning hours. Temperatures at the onset of the precipitation remained below freezing. A four to six hour period of freezing rain occurred across inland Erie and most of Crawford County.  Up to a quarter inch of ice accumulation was reported across southern Erie and Crawford Counties.  The ice accumulated mainly on trees and grassy surfaces. An inch or two of snow accumulation was also reported on higher terrain.  Strong southeast winds accompanied the precipitation with gusts as high as 35 mph reported.  Some trees and limbs were toppled by the ice and there were also a few reports of power outages. A few accidents were reported.

Some trees and limbs were toppled by the ice and there were also a few reports of power outages. A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving low pressure system dropped 1 to 3 inches of snow across Berks County, the Lehigh Valley, the Poconos and higher terrain locations in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs. While snowfall amounts were not heavy, it fell heavily briefly and caused a slushy accumulation on less traveled roadways, bridges and overpasses.  Accidents were also reported in Chester County on the Pennsylvania Turnpike west of Downingtown. Temperatures fell below freezing over the higher terrain overnight and untreated roadways were slippery. Snow began falling during the second half of the morning on the 16th, fell at its heaviest during the first half of the afternoon and ended close to 6 p.m. EDT on the 16th. The snow changed to rain in the local Philadelphia area during the afternoon of the 16th. Representative snowfall included 3.0 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County) and Albrightsville (Carbon County), 2.5 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 2.3 inches in West Rockhill Township (Bucks County),  2.2 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 2.0 inches in Hilltown Township (Bucks County) and Summit Hill (Carbon County), 1.6 inches in Bethlehem (Northampton County), 1.5 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 1.1 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.0 inch in Elverson (Chester County) and Souderton (Montgomery County).||The snow was caused by a quick moving low pressure system that moved from the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 16th, reached West Virginia at 2 p.m. EDT on the 16th and moved off the lower Delmarva Peninsula on the evening of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of wraparound moisture from the departing nor'easter and a new upper low disturbance brought accumulating snow to extreme Eastern Pennsylvania overnight on the 7th into the afternoon of the 8th. Snowfall averaged 2 to 4 inches in counties along the Delaware River from Philadelphia north with lesser amounts in counties farther to the west.||Intermittent precipitation started again on the 7th as rain with some wet snow mixed in over the Poconos. As the precipitation intensity increased, the rain changed to snow in the Poconos during the late afternoon on the 7th, but took most of the overnight for it to occur in the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania. The snow fell at its heaviest during the morning commute on the 8th. While the snow persisted into the afternoon across far Eastern Pennsylvania on the 8th (it ended during the morning farther to the west), as intensity lessened and indirect insolation from the March sun prevented much snow from additionally accumulating from the late morning onward.  In fact, the snow changed to rain before ending that afternoon in the local Philadelphia area. ||Representative snowfall included 3.5 inches in Bensalem (Bucks County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 3.0 inches in Furlong (Bucks County) and Hellertown (Northampton County), 2.8 inches in Horsham (Montgomery County) and Somerton (Philadelphia), 2.0 inches in Penndel (Bucks County) and Rockledge (Philadelphia), 1.4 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 1.1 inches in Wayne (Delaware County), 0.6 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 0.2 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

A large subtropical ridge over the western Atlantic and adjacent southeastern United States pumped unseasonably warm air and rich moisture into the Ohio Valley and Mid-Atlantic. This ring-of-fire pattern of moderate instability and strong deep layer wind shear on the northern periphery of the upper-level ridge was conducive for the development of several early-season thunderstorm complexes (QLCS/MCS). The severe storms with embedded bow echoes moved eastward from the Ohio Valley along and south of a strong low-level frontal zone and produced damaging winds and marginally severe hail in central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated around 70 mph knocked down a swath of 75-100 trees in a heavily wooded area at the Sizerville State Park. The damage straddled the Cameron/Potter county line with most of the damage in Cameron County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Thunderstorms over northern Ohio and southeast Ontario tracked eastward across central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening. Moderate instability and  favorable winds aloft maintained the severe storms which produced damaging winds and excessive rainfall. Very wet antecedent conditions coupled with the heavy thunderstorm rains resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down a swath of trees on Schaffer Mountain near the Cambria/Somerset county line. Video evidence along with radar signatures confirmed a gustnado developed on the leading edge of thunderstorm outflow ahead of a squall line that pushed west-to-east into the Laurel Highlands. It was concluded that the gustnado was primarily responsible for the concentrated area of downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCV and broader mid-level trough crossed the central Appalachians and triggered strong to severe thunderstorms from the central ridges into the Susquehanna River Valley during the afternoon and evening. Very high boundary layer moisture and humidity contributed to heavy rainfall/flash flooding and precipitation loading within consolidating convection. While the deep layer flow and shear were somewhat modest, ambient vertical vorticity just ahead of the MCV and low LCLs aided in the development of two weak, short-lived (EF1) tornadoes in Centre and Perry Counties. Deep moisture (PWAT) values around two inches along with relatively slow, south-to-north training cells and bands produced significant flash flooding, particularly in DuBois and Beech Creek which sustained extensive flood damages due localized 4-6+ inch rainfall amounts. Both towns were declared a State of Emergency. The Bald Eagle Creek at Beech Creek Station surged nearly 8 feet in 4 hours and crested just below major flood stage. The creek officially crested at 13.75 feet which was the sixth highest crest on record. An 86-year old man was swept away and killed by the rising flood waters in Beech Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rains produced flash flooding in the Centre region from Pine Grove Mills and State College through Bellefonte and Milesburg. The hardest hit area was from Milesburg northeast along Route 150 to the Blanchard/Beech Creek Area near the Centre/Clinton county line. Numerous roads were closed including Route 26 from Porter Road to Benner Pike in College Township, Jacksonville Road (Route 26) in Marion Township, Route 3005 (Fox Hollow Road) from Cricklewood Drive in Patton Township to Park Ave/Porter Road in College Township, Route 455 in Walker Township, Route 550 from Upper Gyp Road to Reynolds Lane in Benner Township and Route 26 at Walnut Ridge Road in Marion Township.

The heavy rains and flood waters inundated the Beech Creek area, which resulted in a State of Emergency and Disaster Declaration due to numerous roads closed/damaged and covered in debris from flood waters. Many of the road closures and damage was confined in Beech Creek Township. More than 30 miles of roadway, including drainage culverts and bridges, were affected by the flooding. ||One home in the Beech Creek area was destroyed and another sustained major damage. An additional 14 homes also sustained minor damage. Flood-related damages totaled over 10 million dollars. ||An 86-year-old man was swept away by rising flood waters along Bald Eagle Creek while trying to rescue his ATV. His body was found two days later on Saturday, June 29, 2013 by kayakers. ||The Bald Eagle Creek at Beech Creek Station surged nearly 8 feet in 4 hours and crested just below major flood stage. The creek officially crested at 13.75 feet which was the sixth highest crest on record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the upper Ohio Valley spreading warm and moist air across the region.  Strong to severe thunderstorms developed in association with this front.  A couple of the thunderstorms became severe.  Two tornadoes occurred in  southern Erie County and caused some damage.  A motorcyclist hit a downed tree in western Crawford County and died from his injuries.  ||A second wave of thunderstorms occurred during the evening of the 29th as the warm front lingered over the region.  A thunderstorm became severe in Crawford County and caused some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second tornado touched down about two miles north of Edinboro.  This tornado then traveled east to Union city and finally lifted about three miles southeast of the town.  The tornado appears to have been on the ground only intermittently.  The damage path was almost 18 miles in length and up to 150 yards in width.  The tornado caused considerable tree damage in Union City.  Trees were uprooted and multiple trees fell on homes causing additional damage.  Some of the uprooted trees damaged road surfaces.  The strong winds from the tornado also caused damage to roofs and siding.   A building along Main Street lost it's brick facade.   A couple of homes west of Union City lost large sections of roofing.  A couple of farm buildings were also damaged.  Damage elsewhere along the damage path consisted of mainly downed trees.  Peak winds were estimated around 105 mph making this an EF1 tornado.  Two minor injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires in Blain. This rotating storm produced an EF1 tornado west-southwest of Blain near Big Spring State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCV and broader mid-level trough crossed the central Appalachians and triggered strong to severe thunderstorms from the central ridges into the Susquehanna River Valley during the afternoon and evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rains inundated the DuBois area with over 5 inches of rain in a 2-3 hour period from late morning into the early afternoon. Nearly 6.5 inches of rain fell at the city's sewage treatment plant, which was consistent with radar-derived storm total precipitation estimates.  This resulted in historic flash flooding and closed all roads coming into and going out of the DuBois area. A state of emergency and disaster declaration was issued for Clearfield County and the DuBois area in the wake of widespread flooding. Preliminary damages related to the flooding are estimated around $15 million dollars. ||The flash flooding closed dozens of roads in the northwestern portion of Clearfield County and the DuBois area. The road closures included Route 255 (East DuBois Avenue) from the intersection with McCracken Run Road in DuBois City to Penfield, Route 4003 (Mine Road) between the intersection of Route 410 (Shamokin Trail) and the intersection with Route 4002 (Stanley Bottom Road) in Brady Township, Route 3009 (McGees Mills Road) between the intersection with Buhite Road and the intersection with Grant Road in Brady Township, Dixon Avenue, Jared Street, East Maloney Road near the railroad tracks, Simpson Avenue, Platt Road, Clear Run Road and Pifer Street. DuBois City Manager John Suplizio said that the whole city was hit hard but the lower parts were devastated. Those areas include Chestnut Street, Dixon Avenue, Jared Street, The Flats, Pentz Run, Daly Street, Locust Street, Hamor Street, Sandy Street, DuBois Street and adjacent areas.||Sandy Lick Creek and Tannery Dam overflowed which contributed to the widespread flooding and road closures. The Sandy Township swift water rescue team along with other local fire departments performed over 30 rescues. More than 130 people were taken to the DuBois Area High School where a shelter was set up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the upper Ohio Valley spreading warm and moist air across the region.  Strong to severe thunderstorms developed in association with this front.  A couple of the thunderstorms became severe.  Two tornadoes occurred in  southern Erie County and caused some damage.  A motorcyclist hit a downed tree in western Crawford County and died from his injuries.  ||A second wave of thunderstorms occurred during the evening of the 29th as the warm front lingered over the region.  A thunderstorm became severe in Crawford County and caused some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a large tree on Adamsville Road between Jamestown and Adamsville.  A motorcyclist hit the downed tree just before 9 pm and sustained fatal injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong northwest flow around an anomalously strong (for late May) low pressure system slowly moving through the New England coastal waters and larger tidal spreads associated with the full moon caused pockets of blowout tides to occur during both low tide cycles on the 25th along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries. The lowest tide in Philadelphia reached 1.69 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon along and to the east of the north-central Appalachians. In a very warm/moist and unstable environment, the storms congealed/consolidated surface cold pools and become severe - producing isolated large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down numerous trees along Route 272 near the Lancaster/Chester county-line. The damage occurred near the intersection of Kirks Mill Road and Nottingham Road, which becomes West Christine Road entering Chester County. The road was closed about 100 yards into Chester County due to the downed trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado was confirmed just east of Boalsburg on Nittany Farm between US 322 (General Potter Highway) and Route 45 (Earlystown Road). The maximum wind speed was 100 mph. Eyewitnesses said the tornado pulled a small tree in a holding pond about 50 feet in the air. Visible damages included sporadic trees snapped/knocked down and metal roofing off of several outbuildings and corn crib. The last observed damage was near Cedar Run road or about 1 mile to the northwest from the initial touchdown. There were no injuries or deaths.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced two tornadoes in Erie County.  The first tornado touched down about two miles northeast of Cranesville.  The tornado was on the ground for over a mile and a half and downed many trees.  A mobile home was also overturned and destroyed.  Five people inside the mobile home were injured.  A couple of farm buildings were also heavily damaged.   Peak winds were estimated to be around 90 mph making it an EF1 on the Enhanced Fujita scale.  The damage path was no more than 50 yards wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A week after a flash flood even in eastern Erie County, another heavy rain event came through with 3 to 5 inches of rain. The hardest hit areas were Summit, McKean, and Greene Townships in eastern Erie County. The City of Erie also suffered some flash flooding as a major thruway was cut off by high water. Four hikers were rescued in a gorge as the creek rose quickly, but no injuries were reported. The rain event was caused by short wave trough over the region, with northwest Pennsylvania situated in the warm sector with dew points in the lower 60s. Some overrunning, training, and lift associated with the higher topography in the region supported high rainfall amounts which fell over an 18 hour period. Rainfall from the week before kept the soil conditions wet, and flash flood guidance was as low as 0.65 for 1 hour rainfall during the event. The observed rainfall rates were not notable, however one observer reported over 2/hr at one point when a heavier cell moved over Greene Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measured between 4 and 5 inches feel over Greene, Summit, and McKean Townships in eastern Erie County on the 6th. This heavy rain, over saturated ground, produced widespread flooding. Elk Creek in McKean Township came out of it's banks and inundated a basement and garage at two different homes on Rick Road. Hamot Road was washed out on one side requiring road crews to make repairs. The water depth was estimated at a depth of three feet on some roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCV and broader mid-level trough crossed the central Appalachians and triggered strong to severe thunderstorms from the central ridges into the Susquehanna River Valley during the afternoon and evening. Very high boundary layer moisture and humidity contributed to heavy rainfall/flash flooding and precipitation loading within consolidating convection. While the deep layer flow and shear were somewhat modest, ambient vertical vorticity just ahead of the MCV and low LCLs aided in the development of two weak, short-lived (EF1) tornadoes in Centre and Perry Counties. Deep moisture (PWAT) values around two inches along with relatively slow, south-to-north training cells and bands produced significant flash flooding, particularly in DuBois and Beech Creek which sustained extensive flood damages due localized 4-6+ inch rainfall amounts. Both towns were declared a State of Emergency. The Bald Eagle Creek at Beech Creek Station surged nearly 8 feet in 4 hours and crested just below major flood stage. The creek officially crested at 13.75 feet which was the sixth highest crest on record. An 86-year old man was swept away and killed by the rising flood waters in Beech Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees on Pine Grove Mountain.

Occasional tree damage occurred in the woods along (on the east-side of) Route 274 to the Eby Cemetery. Some trees fell and blocked the road. A metal roof was partially removed from an old barn. A wind circulation was evident in a tall grass field at the end of the 3-mile damage path. All together, about 75 trees were knocked down. There were no injuries or deaths.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornadic storm moved through Union City on the evening of the 28th. Heavy rain accompanied this storm with a 15 minute ASOS rain gage reading of 0.65. The storm total rain the river basin that Union City resides in was approximately 4. Significant flooding accompanied the tornado damage in Union City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Union City resides between two branches of the French Creek. The West Branch of the French Creek is located to the north and west of town and has a reservoir just before the confluence with the southern branch. The reservoir was constructed to retain excess runoff, mainly from spring snow melt. The reservoir water levels jumped over 30 feet as a result of the rain from this storm. Despite this, the southern branch of the French Creek, which runs through the southern part of town, came up quick and high. No gages are available on that tributary, but most of the flood damage was along this creek. The flooding that resulted was widespread through Union Township and the borough. Due to a road washout on Odell Street Ext. There were 7 rescues conducted with use of the fire truck ladder extended across the raging water. The flood damage was estimated over $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was in place across Lake Erie and northwestern Pennsylvania from January 21 to January 24 resulting in periods of heavy lake effect snow in the snowbelt. Minor synoptic snow began on the night of January 20th with snow increasing during the early morning hours of January 21st as west to east lake effect snow bands developed along the lakeshore. Snow intensified on the 21st with a dominant west to east band bringing extremely heavy snow across Erie County. Snowfall rates approached 3 inches per hour for a period of time with near zero visibilities. Snow bands on January 22nd became more transitory, then re-organized again on the evening the of the 22nd.  The snow finally ended on the morning of January 24th.||Some impressive snowfall totals were reported across Erie County with the highest amounts occurring inland from Lake Erie.  Some of the higher amounts included 34.3 inches at Franklin Center; 29.5 inches south of North East; 24.6 inches at Fairview and 24.4 inches in Mill Creek Township.  Officially, 21.8 inches of snow fell at Erie International Airport on the 21st through the 24th with 16.3 inches of that total falling on the 21st.  ||In addition to the snowfall, westerly winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 35 mph persisted on January 21st and 22nd. The combination of arctic air and gusty winds caused dangerous wind chill conditions with values as low as -15 degrees. The gusty winds also caused some blowing and drifting of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was in place across Lake Erie and northwestern Pennsylvania from January 21 to January 24 resulting in periods of heavy lake effect snow in the snowbelt. Snowfall rates approached 3 inches per hour for a period of time with near zero visibilities. The snow finally ended on the morning of January 24th.||Some impressive snowfall totals were reported across Erie County with the highest amounts occurring inland from Lake Erie.  Some of the higher amounts included 34.3 inches at Franklin Center and 29.5 inches south of North East.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced a convective line of showers and scattered thunderstorms with damaging winds that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 1st. About 25,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the greatest concentration in Montgomery, Berks, Lehigh and Monroe Counties.  Most of the power was restored by that afternoon.  The adverse weather caused arrival delays of two hours at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The SEPTA Commuter Regional Rail Airport Line had service suspended until 11 a.m. EDT that morning because of power problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a transformer along Gerald Avenue in Cumru Township.

A wind gust of 53 mph was measured at the nearby Lehigh Valley International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across the Upper Ohio Valley early on January 31st. Cold westerly winds behind the cold front caused lake effect snow showers to develop before daybreak on the 31st. The snow showers intensified around midday with periods of moderate to heavy snow during the afternoon and evening hours. Visibilities were near zero at times with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour. The westerly winds gusted in excess of 30 mph causing much blowing and drifting. Intermittent snow continued into the 1st with the snow shower activity diminishing from west to east during the evening. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 10 inches over most of inland Erie County and northern Crawford County.  In Crawford County, a peak total of 22.3 inches was reported at Conneautville with as much as 12.5 inches in the Meadville area.  In Erie County, peak totals of 12 inches were reported at Franklin Center and to the south of North East.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Hamilton Township. Metropolitan Edison reported about 1,300 of its customers lost power in Monroe County. All power was restored by Noon EDT that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across the Upper Ohio Valley early on January 31st. Cold westerly winds behind the cold front caused lake effect snow showers to develop before daybreak on the 31st. The snow showers intensified around midday with periods of moderate to heavy snow during the afternoon and evening hours. Visibilities were near zero at times with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour. The westerly winds gusted in excess of 30 mph causing much blowing and drifting. Intermittent snow continued into the 1st with the snow shower activity diminishing from west to east during the evening. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 10 inches over most of inland Erie County and northern Crawford County.  In Crawford County, a peak total of 22.3 inches was reported at Conneautville with as much as 12.5 inches in the Meadville area.  In Erie County, peak totals of 12 inches were reported at Franklin Center and to the south of North East.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across the Upper Ohio Valley early on January 31st. Cold westerly winds behind the cold front caused lake effect snow showers to develop before daybreak on the 31st. The snow showers intensified around midday with periods of moderate to heavy snow during the afternoon and evening hours. Visibilities were near zero at times with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour. The westerly winds gusted in excess of 30 mph causing much blowing and drifting.  In Crawford County, a peak total of 22.3 inches was reported at Conneautville with as much as 12.5 inches in the Meadville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was in place across Lake Erie and northwestern Pennsylvania from January 21 to January 24 resulting in periods of heavy lake effect snow in the snowbelt. Minor synoptic snow began on the night of January 20th with snow increasing during the early morning hours of January 21st as west to east lake effect snow bands developed along the lakeshore. Snow intensified on the 21st with a dominant west to east band bringing extremely heavy snow across Erie County. Snowfall rates approached 3 inches per hour for a period of time with near zero visibilities. Snow bands on January 22nd became more transitory, then re-organized again on the evening the of the 22nd.  The snow finally ended on the morning of January 24th.||Some impressive snowfall totals were reported across Erie County with the highest amounts occurring inland from Lake Erie.  Some of the higher amounts included 34.3 inches at Franklin Center; 29.5 inches south of North East; 24.6 inches at Fairview and 24.4 inches in Mill Creek Township.  Officially, 21.8 inches of snow fell at Erie International Airport on the 21st through the 24th with 16.3 inches of that total falling on the 21st.  ||In addition to the snowfall, westerly winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 35 mph persisted on January 21st and 22nd. The combination of arctic air and gusty winds caused dangerous wind chill conditions with values as low as -15 degrees. The gusty winds also caused some blowing and drifting of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was in place across Lake Erie and northwestern Pennsylvania from January 21 to January 24 resulting in periods of heavy lake effect snow in the snowbelt. Snowfall rates approached 3 inches per hour for a period of time with near zero visibilities. The snow finally ended on the morning of January 24th.||Some impressive snowfall totals were reported across Erie County. Some of the higher amounts included 24.6 inches at Fairview and 24.4 inches in Mill Creek Township.  Officially, 21.8 inches of snow fell at Erie International Airport with 16.3 inches of that total falling on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwest flow following a cold frontal passage produced wind gusts as high as around 45 mph in Eastern Pennsylvania into the afternoon on the 24th. The strong winds knocked down weak trees limbs and power lines and also hampered firefighter efforts extinguishing wildfires.  Peak wind gusts included 49 mph in Newtown Square (Delaware County), 44 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 43 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County).

Low pressure originating along the Gulf Coast on November 26th tracked northeast up the east side of the Appalachians during the early hours of November 27th. Heavy snow overspread northwestern Pennsylvania as moisture associated with this system interacted with cold air over the lower Great Lakes. Snow began late in the morning of the 26th and continued into the morning hours of the 27th. After a brief lull, lake effect snow showers developed during the afternoon of 27th and then continued into the 28th.   The snow was heavy at times, especially during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 26th when snowfall rates were in excess of an inch per hour.  Snowfall totals during this event ranged from around six inches over most of inland Erie County to more than a foot over eastern Crawford County.  In Erie County, a peak total of 8.8 inches was measured in Amity Township.  The highest totals in Crawford County were 16.4 inches at Titusville and 13.2 inches at Canadohta Lake.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph at times causing blowing and drifting. Travel was difficult during this event and many accidents were reported.

Travel was difficult during this event and many accidents were reported.

A low pressure system brought mainly snow to the Poconos, a wintry mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain to the Lehigh Valley and some freezing rain and sleet to Berks County and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs overnight on the 15th. Snowfall in the Poconos averaged 3 to 6 inches and in the Lehigh Valley around one inch. Ice accumulations in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the northern and far western Philadelphia suburbs averaged one to two tenths of an inch. The wintry weather made the morning commute on the 16th hazardous and longer than average, especially if traveling on untreated roadways.||In the Poconos, snow began during the late evening of the 15th and ended around 7 a.m. EST on the 16th. In the Lehigh Valley, precipitation started as sleet and rain. As temperatures dropped overnight and precipitation intensity increased, precipitation fell as either snow or freezing rain. Toward morning on the 16th, precipitation fell as freezing rain and the steady precipitation ended in the middle of the morning. In the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, precipitation started as rain. As temperatures dropped overnight and precipitation intensity increased, precipitation fell as either sleet or freezing rain. Toward morning on the 16th, precipitation changed back to plain rain or plain rain mixed with sleet at times. The steady precipitation ended in the middle of the morning.||Representative snowfall included 5.3 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 4.6 inches in East Stroudsburg, 4.5 inches in Summit Hill (Carbon County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Saylorsburg (Monore County), 3.9 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.8 inches in Moorestown (Northampton County), 1.0 inch in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 0.8 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County) and 0.5 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. Ice accumulations included 0.20 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County).||The wintry weather was caused by a series of low pressure systems that developed along a frontal boundary in the southeastern United States on the morning of the 15th. The lows consolidated into one just east of Wilmington, North Carolina overnight on the 15th and then moved northeast. By 7 a.m. EST on the 18th, the low was located around one hundred miles east of the New Jersey coast. The northeasterly flow around that storm track prevented warmer air at the surface (and in the Poconos aloft also) from ever moving farther north than Philadelphia and its immediate suburbs.

Heavy rainfall between 2 and 3 inches combined with cold season hydrological effects produced widespread poor-drainage and small stream flooding across Adams, York and Lancaster counties. Numerous roads were closed and several water rescues were reported. Minor flood stages were exceed on the Swatara Creek at Harper Tavern and the Conestoga River at Lancaster.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 2 and 3 inches caused significant flooding across the county. Numerous roads were closed and multiple water rescues were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A return southwest flow of relatively milder air caused a light accumulating snow (near an inch) to occur in Southeast Pennsylvania during the early morning of the 6th. The combination of the snow itself and temperatures falling to or below the freezing mark once the snow began falling caused slippery travel to occur mainly on untreated less traveled roadways as well as bridges and overpasses. The snow fell between 2 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST on the 6th.  Representative snowfall included 0.8 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 0.7 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and Chadds Ford (Delaware County), 0.6 inches in Chester Spring (Chester County) and 0.5 inches in Lower Makefield Township (Bucks County).

A strong upper level trough crossed the central Appalachians and induced an area of low pressure over the Piedmont region of southeastern Virginia/northeastern North Carolina. The low moved slowly to the northeast across the southern tip of the Delmarva Peninsula and off the Mid-Atlantic coast. This storm produced moderate to heavy snowfall to the north and west of its track, with snowfall amounts ranging between 5 and 10 inches across far southwestern and south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were observed from just after midnight through the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow cold frontal rain band (NCFRB) crossed central Pennsylvania during the morning hours and produced isolated wind damage. The high shear/low cape environment was indicative low-topped convection along the sharp surface front, accentuated by pre-frontal south/southeasterly flow in excess of 50kts. Instability was the primary limiting factor concerning severe weather potential, with considerable warm-sector cloud cover inhibiting surface heating. The colder air aloft lagged behind the narrow convective band, which lead to weak low-mid tropospheric lapse rates and further reduced the potential for the strong low level winds/momentum from being transferred down to the surface. Precipitation loading was the primary mechanism for mixing down the strong low level winds, which seemed to be largely ineffective given the general lack of concentrated wind damage reports for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Main and North Centre Townships between Bloomsburg and Berwick.

A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening. Snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain/drizzle with a glaze of ice topping storm total snow accumulations between 4 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced, narrow line of heavy showers raced eastward across the area during the predawn and early morning hours. Despite limited instability and no lightning detected with the shallow convection, the presence of an intense wind field in excess of 50 knots just above the surface promoted corridors of wind damage with the passage of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers with estimated winds near 60 mph knocked down 15 trees in the Johnstown area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system riding along a cold front prolonged precipitation long enough that it changed to freezing rain and then snow before ending in the Poconos overnight on the 6th.  Precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of the 6th. As colder air started working its way into the region, the rain started transitioning to freezing rain during the evening of the 6th. Overnight the freezing rain changed to sleet and then snow before ending early on the 7th. The combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain accumulated around one quarter of an inch in the higher terrain with less amounts in the valleys. Untreated roadways and walkways were hazardous.

A complex low pressure system tracked across the Great Lakes during the 8th and the 9th, allowing an associated frontal boundary to move over the northern Mid Atlantic Region on the 8th, with a secondary area of low pressure forming off the Mid Atlantic coast. Significant forcing and lift within the atmosphere resulted in an intense band of snow developing across northern Maryland during the morning of the 8th. This heavy snow band pushed east, moving over portions of northern Maryland, northern Delaware, southeastern Pennsylvania, and into central to southern New Jersey during the afternoon. Heavy snow fell for several hours, and areas under this banding feature received in excess of 6 inches of snow, with the highest snowfall amounts reaching 12 inches. Snowfall rates within this intense snow band reached 2 to 3 inches per hour for a time. Outside of this snow band and its effected area, most locations received between 1 and 4 inches of snow, with mainly less than an inch falling across northern New Jersey and southern parts of the Delmarva. ||By late afternoon to the evening of the 8th, the stronger forcing and banding of precipitation diminished, but with warmer air moving into the region, a transition to sleet, freezing rain, and rain occurred from southeast to northwest into the night of the 8th and the early morning of the 9th. The transition to rain occurred more quickly across the Delmarva and into portions of southern New Jersey, while cold air lingering at the surface resulted in freezing rain lasting into the night from around the Lehigh Valley, the Poconos, and into northwest New Jersey. Ice accretion was reported across the area, with ice accumulations in the one tenth to one quarter inch range, mainly from the Interstate 95 corridor on north and westward.||Representative snowfall totals included 10.5 inches in Garnet Valley (Delaware County), 8.6 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia County), 8.0 inches in Aldan (Delaware County) and in Springfield (Delaware County), 7.8 inches in Kennett Square (Chester County), 7.1 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 6.5 inches in West Grove (Chester County), 5.5 inches in Center City Philadelphia (Philadelphia County) and in Kulpsville (Montgomery County), 4.5 inches in Franklin Township (Carbon County), 4.0 inches in Rockledge (Philadelphia County) and in Harleysville (Montgomery County), 3.7 inches in Bensalem (Bucks County), 2.5 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 2.0 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 1.8 inches in Reading (Berks County), 1.5 inches in Oley (Berks County) and in Bernville (Berks County), 1.3 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport in Allentown (Lehigh County) and in Heidelberg Township (Lehigh County), 1.2 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 1.0 inches in Chapman (Carbon County), and 0.7 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County). ||Representative ice accumulations included two tenths of an inch in Albrightsville (Carbon County) and in Forks Township (Northampton County) and also in Spring City (Chester County), and one tenth of an inch in Blakeslee (Carbon County) and in Martins Creek (Northampton County) and also in Malvern (Chester County) and in Perkasie (Bucks County) and also in Pottstown and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County).  ||The heavy snow caused numerous accidents and road closures in southeastern Pennsylvania. A 35-year-old man was killed near Morgantown (Berks County) after he left his car and walked on the right embankment toward the most severe-looking accident. He was struck and killed when another vehicle swerved onto the embankment to avoid hitting people standing on the roadway.  This fatal accident was part of multiple accidents along a twelve mile stretch of the westbound Pennsylvania Turnpike that involved fifty vehicles. One other serious injury occurred.  The westbound lanes were closed for six hours between Downingtown and Morgantown.  In Philadelphia, the Platt Street Bridge (Pennsylvania State Route 291) was closed for a few hours that afternoon as was northbound Interstate 95 at Columbus Boulevard.  Multiple snarling accidents occurred in Delaware County. Southbound Interstate 95 was also closed for five hours near U.S. Route 322. A 10 vehicle accident occurred on U.S. Route 1 in Marple Township. Accidents were also reported in Nether Providence, Media, Springfield, Clifton Heights and Darby.  In Bucks County, Street Road (Pennsylvania State Route 132) was gridlocked. Snow related problems were not just limited to roadways in the local Philadelphia area. Philadelphia International Airport experienced delays of up to four and a half hours as only one runway was kept open and a frozen switch caused the suspension of SEPTA Regional Rail service on the Manayunk/Norristown Line.

A strong upper level trough crossed the central Appalachians and induced an area of low pressure over the Piedmont region of southeastern Virginia/northeastern North Carolina. The low moved slowly to the northeast across the southern tip of the Delmarva Peninsula and off the Mid-Atlantic coast. This storm produced moderate to heavy snowfall to the north and west of its track, with snowfall amounts ranging between 5 and 10 inches across far southwestern and south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were observed from just after midnight through the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening. Snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain/drizzle with a glaze of ice topping storm total snow accumulations between 4 and 8 inches.

A strong upper level trough crossed the central Appalachians and induced an area of low pressure over the Piedmont region of southeastern Virginia/northeastern North Carolina. The low moved slowly to the northeast across the southern tip of the Delmarva Peninsula and off the Mid-Atlantic coast. This storm produced moderate to heavy snowfall to the north and west of its track, with snowfall amounts ranging between 5 and 10 inches across far southwestern and south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 inches were observed mainly over the southern half of the county. Sporadic power outages were reported due to heavy wet snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow cold frontal rain band (NCFRB) crossed central Pennsylvania during the morning hours and produced isolated wind damage. The high shear/low cape environment was indicative low-topped convection along the sharp surface front, accentuated by pre-frontal south/southeasterly flow in excess of 50kts. Instability was the primary limiting factor concerning severe weather potential, with considerable warm-sector cloud cover inhibiting surface heating. The colder air aloft lagged behind the narrow convective band, which lead to weak low-mid tropospheric lapse rates and further reduced the potential for the strong low level winds/momentum from being transferred down to the surface. Precipitation loading was the primary mechanism for mixing down the strong low level winds, which seemed to be largely ineffective given the general lack of concentrated wind damage reports for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along RT 30 east of Chambersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening. Snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain/drizzle with a glaze of ice topping storm total snow accumulations between 4 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A return southwest flow of relatively milder air caused a light accumulating snow (near an inch) to occur in Southeast Pennsylvania during the early morning of the 6th. The combination of the snow itself and temperatures falling to or below the freezing mark once the snow began falling caused slippery travel to occur mainly on untreated less traveled roadways as well as bridges and overpasses. The snow fell between 2 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST on the 6th.  Representative snowfall included 0.8 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 0.7 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and Chadds Ford (Delaware County), 0.6 inches in Chester Spring (Chester County) and 0.5 inches in Lower Makefield Township (Bucks County).

A low pressure system brought mainly snow to the Poconos, a wintry mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain to the Lehigh Valley and some freezing rain and sleet to Berks County and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs overnight on the 15th. Snowfall in the Poconos averaged 3 to 6 inches and in the Lehigh Valley around one inch. Ice accumulations in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the northern and far western Philadelphia suburbs averaged one to two tenths of an inch. The wintry weather made the morning commute on the 16th hazardous and longer than average, especially if traveling on untreated roadways.||In the Poconos, snow began during the late evening of the 15th and ended around 7 a.m. EST on the 16th. In the Lehigh Valley, precipitation started as sleet and rain. As temperatures dropped overnight and precipitation intensity increased, precipitation fell as either snow or freezing rain. Toward morning on the 16th, precipitation fell as freezing rain and the steady precipitation ended in the middle of the morning. In the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, precipitation started as rain. As temperatures dropped overnight and precipitation intensity increased, precipitation fell as either sleet or freezing rain. Toward morning on the 16th, precipitation changed back to plain rain or plain rain mixed with sleet at times. The steady precipitation ended in the middle of the morning.||Representative snowfall included 5.3 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 4.6 inches in East Stroudsburg, 4.5 inches in Summit Hill (Carbon County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Saylorsburg (Monore County), 3.9 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.8 inches in Moorestown (Northampton County), 1.0 inch in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 0.8 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County) and 0.5 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. Ice accumulations included 0.20 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County).||The wintry weather was caused by a series of low pressure systems that developed along a frontal boundary in the southeastern United States on the morning of the 15th. The lows consolidated into one just east of Wilmington, North Carolina overnight on the 15th and then moved northeast. By 7 a.m. EST on the 18th, the low was located around one hundred miles east of the New Jersey coast. The northeasterly flow around that storm track prevented warmer air at the surface (and in the Poconos aloft also) from ever moving farther north than Philadelphia and its immediate suburbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very persistent and strong northwest flow that started during the evening of the 23rd caused blowout tide conditions during the daytime low tide cycle on the 24th along tidal sections of the Delaware River in Pennsylvania. In Philadelphia, the low tide reached 2.34 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

A low pressure system emerging from the Gulf of Mexico combining with a cold air mass in place produced a wintry mix of precipitation at the onset of the event across the far northwest Philadelphia suburbs, Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos on the 26th. The most protracted wintry precipitation occurred on the Pocono Plateau where a couple of inches of snow was followed by a couple of tenths of an inch of ice.  South of the Poconos, most of the wintry precipitation was in the form of freezing rain as pockets of ice were reported as far south as some of the Philadelphia northwest suburbs. In the Poconos, precipitation started as snow during the early morning of the 26th and changed over to sleet and then freezing rain during the afternoon of the 26th. Precipitation changed over to plain rain late that evening. Elsewhere, precipitation started briefly as snow and sleet on the morning of the 26th before changing over to rain and freezing rain. Temperatures hovered right around the freezing mark, so freezing rain was elevation dependent. Enough warm air moved in by the early evening of the 26th to change the precipitation over to plain rain.  Until the changeover, untreated roadways were hazardous. In Monroe County, dozens of cars skidded off roadways, but no serious injuries were reported. ||Snowfall included 3.1 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 0.5 inches in Pocono Summit and Reeders in Monroe County and in Albrightsville (Carbon County).  Ice accumulations included 0.20 in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) and 0.15 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County).| |The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that emerged from the Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 26th. The low tracked northeastward through the Florida Panhandle on the afternoon of the 26th and into the Carolinas on the evening of the 26th.  Enough cold air was initially in place to cause the wintry mix until the circulation around the low brought in sufficient warm air near the surface from the Atlantic Ocean.

A strong cold front produced a convective line of showers and scattered thunderstorms with damaging winds that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 1st. About 25,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the greatest concentration in Montgomery, Berks, Lehigh and Monroe Counties.  Most of the power was restored by that afternoon.  The adverse weather caused arrival delays of two hours at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The SEPTA Commuter Regional Rail Airport Line had service suspended until 11 a.m. EDT that morning because of power problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires in Robeson Township. Wires fell onto the garage of one home on Old River Road. No damage to the garage was reported.  Pennsylvania Power and Light and Metropolitan Edison reported about 2,000 of their customers lost power in Berks County. Power was restored by Noon EDT that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening. Snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain/drizzle with a glaze of ice topping storm total snow accumulations between 4 and 8 inches.
