EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system emerging from the Gulf of Mexico combining with a cold air mass in place produced a wintry mix of precipitation at the onset of the event across the far northwest Philadelphia suburbs, Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos on the 26th. The most protracted wintry precipitation occurred on the Pocono Plateau where a couple of inches of snow was followed by a couple of tenths of an inch of ice.  South of the Poconos, most of the wintry precipitation was in the form of freezing rain as pockets of ice were reported as far south as some of the Philadelphia northwest suburbs. In the Poconos, precipitation started as snow during the early morning of the 26th and changed over to sleet and then freezing rain during the afternoon of the 26th. Precipitation changed over to plain rain late that evening. Elsewhere, precipitation started briefly as snow and sleet on the morning of the 26th before changing over to rain and freezing rain. Temperatures hovered right around the freezing mark, so freezing rain was elevation dependent. Enough warm air moved in by the early evening of the 26th to change the precipitation over to plain rain.  Until the changeover, untreated roadways were hazardous. In Monroe County, dozens of cars skidded off roadways, but no serious injuries were reported. ||Snowfall included 3.1 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 0.5 inches in Pocono Summit and Reeders in Monroe County and in Albrightsville (Carbon County).  Ice accumulations included 0.20 in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) and 0.15 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County).| |The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that emerged from the Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 26th. The low tracked northeastward through the Florida Panhandle on the afternoon of the 26th and into the Carolinas on the evening of the 26th.  Enough cold air was initially in place to cause the wintry mix until the circulation around the low brought in sufficient warm air near the surface from the Atlantic Ocean.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought mainly snow to the Poconos, a wintry mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain to the Lehigh Valley and some freezing rain and sleet to Berks County and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs overnight on the 15th. Snowfall in the Poconos averaged 3 to 6 inches and in the Lehigh Valley around one inch. Ice accumulations in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the northern and far western Philadelphia suburbs averaged one to two tenths of an inch. The wintry weather made the morning commute on the 16th hazardous and longer than average, especially if traveling on untreated roadways.||In the Poconos, snow began during the late evening of the 15th and ended around 7 a.m. EST on the 16th. In the Lehigh Valley, precipitation started as sleet and rain. As temperatures dropped overnight and precipitation intensity increased, precipitation fell as either snow or freezing rain. Toward morning on the 16th, precipitation fell as freezing rain and the steady precipitation ended in the middle of the morning. In the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, precipitation started as rain. As temperatures dropped overnight and precipitation intensity increased, precipitation fell as either sleet or freezing rain. Toward morning on the 16th, precipitation changed back to plain rain or plain rain mixed with sleet at times. The steady precipitation ended in the middle of the morning.||Representative snowfall included 5.3 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 4.6 inches in East Stroudsburg, 4.5 inches in Summit Hill (Carbon County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Saylorsburg (Monore County), 3.9 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.8 inches in Moorestown (Northampton County), 1.0 inch in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 0.8 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County) and 0.5 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. Ice accumulations included 0.20 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County).||The wintry weather was caused by a series of low pressure systems that developed along a frontal boundary in the southeastern United States on the morning of the 15th. The lows consolidated into one just east of Wilmington, North Carolina overnight on the 15th and then moved northeast. By 7 a.m. EST on the 18th, the low was located around one hundred miles east of the New Jersey coast. The northeasterly flow around that storm track prevented warmer air at the surface (and in the Poconos aloft also) from ever moving farther north than Philadelphia and its immediate suburbs.

A complex frontal system produced moderate to heavy snow and mixed precipitation over portions of south-central Pennsylvania. Light snow developed during the early morning hours with a several-hour lull in the wintry precipitation from mid-morning into the early afternoon. The period of most intense snow and accompanying white-out conditions occurred from the mid-afternoon through the early evening. The snow ended during the late evening on the 18th with a period of light freezing drizzle tapering off during the overnight hours on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations around 6 inches were observed mainly over the northern half of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening. Snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain/drizzle with a glaze of ice topping storm total snow accumulations between 3 and 6 inches. The mixed wintry precipitation adversely impacted travel especially along the PA Turnpike and I-70 corridors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system with energy transferring from the Ohio Valley across the Central Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic coast produced widespread snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches over much of central Pennsylvania. The snow overspread the area from southwest to northeast and became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening, with snowfall rates between 1 and locally 2 inches per hour. By late in the evening, strong low-level warm advection resulted in above freezing temperatures aloft and caused a changeover from snow to sleet and freezing rain, with significant ice accretions observed in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation tapered off to light snow and freezing drizzle around midnight in most locations. The heavy snow topped by a layer of ice sponsored dangerous travel conditions with numerous vehicle accidents involving passenger cars and jackknifed tractor-trailers resulting in several major road closures including portions of I-80, I-81 and US 322. There were two indirect storm-related fatalities, where two people died in a head-on collision on SR 4002 in Blair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow started in the morning and became heavy at times through the afternoon and evening. Snow changed to sleet and then freezing rain/drizzle with a quarter inch of ice topping storm total snow accumulations between 3 and 6 inches. The mixed wintry precipitation adversely impacted travel especially along the PA Turnpike and I-81 corridors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced a convective line of showers and scattered thunderstorms with damaging winds that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 1st. About 25,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the greatest concentration in Montgomery, Berks, Lehigh and Monroe Counties.  Most of the power was restored by that afternoon.  The adverse weather caused arrival delays of two hours at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The SEPTA Commuter Regional Rail Airport Line had service suspended until 11 a.m. EDT that morning because of power problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms with damaging winds knocked down numerous trees and wires across Carbon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front generated a line of showers and isolated thunderstorms that produced widespread wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph as it moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning of the 18th. It also caused stronger, but isolated, damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The convective line knocked down large tree limbs and wires and caused power outages in Lehighton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought mainly snow to the Poconos, a wintry mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain to the Lehigh Valley and some freezing rain and sleet to Berks County and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs overnight on the 15th. Snowfall in the Poconos averaged 3 to 6 inches and in the Lehigh Valley around one inch. Ice accumulations in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the northern and far western Philadelphia suburbs averaged one to two tenths of an inch. The wintry weather made the morning commute on the 16th hazardous and longer than average, especially if traveling on untreated roadways.||In the Poconos, snow began during the late evening of the 15th and ended around 7 a.m. EST on the 16th. In the Lehigh Valley, precipitation started as sleet and rain. As temperatures dropped overnight and precipitation intensity increased, precipitation fell as either snow or freezing rain. Toward morning on the 16th, precipitation fell as freezing rain and the steady precipitation ended in the middle of the morning. In the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, precipitation started as rain. As temperatures dropped overnight and precipitation intensity increased, precipitation fell as either sleet or freezing rain. Toward morning on the 16th, precipitation changed back to plain rain or plain rain mixed with sleet at times. The steady precipitation ended in the middle of the morning.||Representative snowfall included 5.3 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 5.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 4.6 inches in East Stroudsburg, 4.5 inches in Summit Hill (Carbon County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Saylorsburg (Monore County), 3.9 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 1.8 inches in Moorestown (Northampton County), 1.0 inch in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 0.8 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County) and 0.5 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. Ice accumulations included 0.20 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County).||The wintry weather was caused by a series of low pressure systems that developed along a frontal boundary in the southeastern United States on the morning of the 15th. The lows consolidated into one just east of Wilmington, North Carolina overnight on the 15th and then moved northeast. By 7 a.m. EST on the 18th, the low was located around one hundred miles east of the New Jersey coast. The northeasterly flow around that storm track prevented warmer air at the surface (and in the Poconos aloft also) from ever moving farther north than Philadelphia and its immediate suburbs.

A strongly forced, narrow line of heavy showers raced eastward across the area during the predawn and early morning hours. Despite limited instability and no lightning detected with the shallow convection, the presence of an intense wind field in excess of 50 knots just above the surface promoted corridors of wind damage with the passage of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers with estimated winds near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires in Irvona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trof in combination with an upper air disturbance triggered clusters of showers and thunderstorms with torrential downpours to occur in southeast Pennsylvania during the late morning into the early afternoon on the 2nd. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front helped trigger a series of thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania on the 12th.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through the area during the late afternoon and early evening on the 12th caused the majority of the wind and lightning damage. About 3,000 Pennsylvania Power and Light customers lost power in the Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Oley Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms preceded a cold frontal passage during the late morning and first half of the afternoon in Eastern Pennsylvania. The strong southerly flow preceding the squall line knocked down a tree in Reading (Berks County). The tree fell along North Fourth Street and damaged a home and a non functioning streetlight.  About 25 Metropolitan Edison customers lost power because of the downed tree. The same strong southerly flow preceding this front coincided with the spring tides associated with the new moon to produce some minor tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River during the afternoon high tide cycle on the 7th. High tide reached 8.40 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia, minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 59 mph within the city of Allentown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass caused high temperatures to reach between 90F and 95F on the 11th. Combined with high dew point levels, afternoon heat index values reached 100F to 105F from Berks County and the Lehigh Valley southeastward through the Philadelphia Metropolitan Area. Maximum temperatures included 94 degrees in Philadelphia and Reading (Berks County), 93 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 92 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 91 degrees in Quakertown (Bucks County).  The core of the hot air mass moved offshore that night, so high temperatures (and heat index values) were slightly lower on the 12th. A cold frontal passage the night of the 12th brought the end of 90 degree days for 2013.

The combination of a lee side trof and an approaching cold front triggered scattered thunderstorms during the day on the 2nd in Eastern Pennsylvania.  While flash flooding caused the majority of the impacts, lightning strikes and severe winds also caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes damaged three homes in Forks Township. The damage was from the lightning strikes themselves as there were no ensuing fires reported.  No serious injuries were also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home and caused a fire in the Frankford section of Philadelphia. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a lee side trof and an approaching cold front triggered scattered thunderstorms during the day on the 2nd in Eastern Pennsylvania.  While flash flooding caused the majority of the impacts, lightning strikes and severe winds also caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down five trees in central Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with the nor'easter that stalled off the Delmarva Peninsula and North Carolina coast caused poor drainage flooding as well as some minor flooding along the Brandywine Basin on the 11th.  Rain began falling overnight on the 9th, continued during the day on the 10th, and fell at its heaviest during the first half of the day on the 11th. The rain ended overnight on the 11th. Event precipitation totals averaged 2.5 to 4.5 inches. Dry antecedent conditions prevented creek flooding from being worse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain that fell earlier on the 11th caused minor flooding along sections of the Main Stem of the Brandywine Creek that persisted into the night of the 11th. The heavy rain also caused poor drainage flooding. The Main Stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford was above its 9 foot flood stage from 138 p.m. EDT on the 11th through 130 a.m. EDT on the 12th. It crested at 9.47 feet at 637 p.m. EDT on the 11th. Event precipitation totals included 3.50 inches in Chadds Ford and 2.03 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain that fell early on the 11th caused minor flooding along sections of the East Branch and Main Stem of the Brandywine Creek that persisted into the night of the 11th.  The heavy rain also caused poor drainage flooding. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 843 a.m. EDT through 228 p.m. EDT on the 11th. It crested at 8.87 feet at 1215 p.m. EDT on the 11th. The Main Stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford was above its 9 foot flood stage from 138 p.m. EDT on the 11th through 130 a.m. EDT on the 12th. It crested at 9.47 feet at 637 p.m. EDT on the 11th. Event precipitation totals included 4.35 inches in Modena, 4.29 inches in Longwood, 3.76 inches in Northbrook and 3.59 inches in Downingtown.

In Springfield Township, flash flooding along the Stony Creek backed into storm drains in the parking lot of BJ's Wholesalers on Woodland Avenue. The water reached up to five feet high and completely submerged some vehicles. About twenty water rescues were performed as fifty vehicles were affected in the parking lot.  Seven fire companies responded to the flooding. Specialized rescue boat water units were used to rescue people from their vehicles. Numerous roadways were flooded in the township and neighboring Upper Darby Township. One vehicle was swept into a flooded creek. The intersections of Normandy and Marshall Roads, Copley and Marshall Roads and Lansdowne Avenue and State Road were all flooded and closed for several hours. In Middletown Township, several water rescues occurred in the Lima area.  A Skywarn spotter also reported flooding along Cobbs Creek bordering Philadelphia.   Event precipitation totals included 3.59 inches in Swarthmore, 2.85 inches in Havertown, 2.00 inches in Drexel Hill and 1.89 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms preceded a cold frontal passage during the late morning and first half of the afternoon in Eastern Pennsylvania. The strong southerly flow preceding the squall line knocked down a tree in Reading (Berks County). The tree fell along North Fourth Street and damaged a home and a non functioning streetlight.  About 25 Metropolitan Edison customers lost power because of the downed tree. The same strong southerly flow preceding this front coincided with the spring tides associated with the new moon to produce some minor tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River during the afternoon high tide cycle on the 7th. High tide reached 8.40 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia, minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms uprooted whole trees in Solebury Township and a couple of downed trees damaged homes. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front helped trigger a series of thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania on the 12th.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through the area during the late afternoon and early evening on the 12th caused the majority of the wind and lightning damage. About 3,000 Pennsylvania Power and Light customers lost power in the Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Muhlenberg Township. One downed tree fell onto and damaged a home. An elderly woman was injured and trapped within her house. She had to be extricated and was transported to Reading Hospital. A wind gust to 46 mph occurred at the Reading Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in and around Allentown during the evening of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second straight day of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused roadway and small creek flash flooding in Allentown and Whitehall Township. Numerous roadways were flooded with some water rescues from trapped vehicles. A child nearly was swept away in the flood waters before being rescued by an adult. Manhole covers popped. About 43 vehicles were badly damaged in the flooding in the West End of Allentown and several businesses were flooded. Flood damage in the area also occurred to homes, garages and basements. No serious injuries were reported. About 2200 people were sheltered in place at the Allentown Fair off of Liberty Street in the city because the entrances and exits to the fairground were flooded. The Zac Brown Band had to cut their concert short. Fair goers were able to leave later that evening.  Damage to the fair was limited to grass and gravel erosion. Elsewhere in the city, vehicles were trapped in flood waters on Sumner Avenue, 22nd and Allen Street, 13th and Liberty Street and 18th and Tilghman Street. About ten homes around 22nd and Allen Street had water in their basements and the electricity was turned off for precautionary reasons.  Event precipitation totals included 1.26 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold air moving across warm Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the evening of October 23rd. The snow briefly tapered off during the middle portion of the 24th and then picked up again during the afternoon. The snow finally ended for good during the morning hours of 25th. The trees across the region still had leaves and the snow caused considerable damage. Hundreds of trees and limbs were downed resulting in scattered power outages. Accumulations of 4 to locally 8 inches were reported across northern Crawford County.  During the peak of the event, thunder and lightning were reported and snow rates approached two inches per hour. It took till October 26th for the power to be fully restored. Many accidents resulted from this early season snow and travel was a problem in some areas.

Very cold air crossing the Great Lakes in northwest flow produced widespread snow across western Pennsylvania, and the higher elevations of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland from the evening of the 23rd into early afternoon of the 24th.  Very heavy snow fell across Venango and Clarion counties in Pennsylvania as a nearly stationary band of snow developed from Lake Huron across Lake Erie with 8-20 inches of snow across northern Venango county, and 6 to 12 inches across portions of Clarion county. Outside of this heavier band 2 to 6 inches of snow fell in the Laurel and Chestnut Ridges, and across northern portions of western Pennsylvania. 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the higher elevations of Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, and in Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy lake effect snow produced 8 to 20 inches of snow across northern portions of Venango county. At the height of the storm a mobile home roof collapsed due to the weight of the snow. More than 1500 power outages were reported, with 32 road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant winter storm of the 2013-2014 winter season brought a mixed bag of snow, sleet, freezing rain, and rain to central Pennsylvania, just in time to adversely impact the pre-Thanksgiving travel.  A strengthening low pressure area over the southeast states on 11/26 moved northeast across NJ on 11/27.  Snow spread into the area rather quickly after midnight on the 26th with light accumulations over the southwest and central areas by mid-day. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the afternoon with freezing rain and rain continuing across much of the area overnight. A transition back to snow was observed later on the 27th before the wintry precipitation ended. Snow accumulations were variable from 1 to 5 inches over central and south-central mountains to in excess of 6 inches in Warren County. Significant icing due to a prolonged period of freezing rain was measured over central and southwest areas with ice accumulations between 0.25 and 0.50 inch. After a relatively brief period of mixed wintry precipitation early in the day on the 26th, eastern areas such as the lower Susquehanna valley picked up between 1.5 to 2 inches of rain from the event. The adverse weather caused hazardous and even dangerous driving conditions across much of the area, resulting in numerous vehicle accidents. The pre-Thanksgiving increase in travel activity was a significant concern. The heavy icing caused trees and limbs to fall bringing down wires and sponsoring power outages.  Many schools were closed over the central and western areas on 11/26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow and freezing rain resulted in significant winter weather impacts during the pre-Thanksgiving holiday travel. These included multiple vehicle accidents, road closures, downed trees/wires and power outages. Snow accumulations by mid-day on the 26th averaged between 1 and 3 inches, followed by a prolonged period of moderate to heavy freezing rain with ice accretion between 0.25 and 0.50 inch by nightfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system over the mid-Atlantic region associated with the remnants of TS Karen brought excessive rainfall (6-10+ inches over two days) and significant flooding to the Lower Susquehanna Valley (LSV). The impacts included widespread significant flooding, numerous closed roads/underpasses, multiple water rescues, evacuations and two residential structure collapses. Fortunately, the area had been abnormally dry prior to the record-breaking rainfall. ||Moderate river flooding was observed on the Yellow Breeches Creek in Camp Hill. Minor river flooding occurred on the Conestoga at Lancaster, Swatara at Hershey, and on the Conodoguinet Creek at Hogestown. Some of the highest storm total rainfall amounts in excess of 10 inches were in the vicinity of Harrisburg. Daily precipitation records were set at Harrisburg International Airport (MDT) on October 10/11. The 2-day excessive rainfall event made October 2013 the wettest October on record at MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall of 5-10 inches over a 2-day period resulted in widespread significant flooding. Numerous roads were closed especially over the eastern and northern portions of the county. Two residential structures collapsed in Oxford and Tyrone Townships.

High tide reached 8.40 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia, minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of severe thunderstorms knocked down a few trees in and around Earl Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold air crossing the Great Lakes in northwest flow produced widespread snow across western Pennsylvania, and the higher elevations of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland from the evening of the 23rd into early afternoon of the 24th.  Very heavy snow fell across Venango and Clarion counties in Pennsylvania as a nearly stationary band of snow developed from Lake Huron across Lake Erie with 8-20 inches of snow across northern Venango county, and 6 to 12 inches across portions of Clarion county. Outside of this heavier band 2 to 6 inches of snow fell in the Laurel and Chestnut Ridges, and across northern portions of western Pennsylvania. 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the higher elevations of Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, and in Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy lake effect snow produced from 6 to 12 inches of snow across portions of Clarion county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant winter storm of the 2013-2014 winter season brought a mixed bag of snow, sleet, freezing rain, and rain to central Pennsylvania, just in time to adversely impact the pre-Thanksgiving travel.  A strengthening low pressure area over the southeast states on 11/26 moved northeast across NJ on 11/27.  Snow spread into the area rather quickly after midnight on the 26th with light accumulations over the southwest and central areas by mid-day. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the afternoon with freezing rain and rain continuing across much of the area overnight. A transition back to snow was observed later on the 27th before the wintry precipitation ended. Snow accumulations were variable from 1 to 5 inches over central and south-central mountains to in excess of 6 inches in Warren County. Significant icing due to a prolonged period of freezing rain was measured over central and southwest areas with ice accumulations between 0.25 and 0.50 inch. After a relatively brief period of mixed wintry precipitation early in the day on the 26th, eastern areas such as the lower Susquehanna valley picked up between 1.5 to 2 inches of rain from the event. The adverse weather caused hazardous and even dangerous driving conditions across much of the area, resulting in numerous vehicle accidents. The pre-Thanksgiving increase in travel activity was a significant concern. The heavy icing caused trees and limbs to fall bringing down wires and sponsoring power outages.  Many schools were closed over the central and western areas on 11/26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow and freezing rain resulted in significant winter weather impacts during the pre-Thanksgiving holiday travel. These included multiple vehicle accidents, road closures, downed trees/wires and power outages. Snow started around 1000 EST with accumulations by late afternoon on the 26th between 2 and 4 inches. Snow continued into the early evening before changing to a period of freezing rain for several hours prior to midnight. The freezing rain eventually changed back to snow with additional lake effect snows into the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms preceded a cold frontal passage during the late morning and first half of the afternoon in Eastern Pennsylvania. The strong southerly flow preceding the squall line knocked down a tree in Reading (Berks County). The tree fell along North Fourth Street and damaged a home and a non functioning streetlight.  About 25 Metropolitan Edison customers lost power because of the downed tree. The same strong southerly flow preceding this front coincided with the spring tides associated with the new moon to produce some minor tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River during the afternoon high tide cycle on the 7th. High tide reached 8.40 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia, minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A  squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms knocked down a couple of trees in East Allen Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trof helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with torrential downpours during the morning of the 9th in the Poconos and Monroe County in particular. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 2 to 6 inches in the southeastern two-thirds of Monroe County. This caused flash flooding of smaller streams as well as poor drainage flooding. The runoff from the heavy rain caused the Delaware River water levels to be higher than average. The next day (the 10th) a 30-year-old man drowned in the river while tubing with about 10 other people off of Kingwood in Hunterdon County. They were tubing across the river toward Tinicum in Bucks County when he went into the river.  Investigators say he was about fifteen feet from the shoreline when he fell off the tube and went missing. Divers searching for the body over the next week were hampered by the high water levels and poor visibilities and additional heavy rain. His body was apparently found on August 31st on a small island in the Delaware River. Lower Makefield (Bucks County) police were called to a report of a body on an island one mile north of the Calhoun Street Bridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage as well as small stream flash flooding in Monroe County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3.5 and 6.0 inches in an axis from Chestnuthill and Tunkhannock Townships northeastward through Price and Middle Smithfield Townships. Measured event precipitation totals included 3.29 inches in Stroudsburg, 3.02 inches in Reeders, 2.57 inches in McMichaels and 2.52 inches in East Stroudsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant instability combined with modest lapse rates and abundant deep-layer moisture (PW) produced numerous thunderstorm clusters with locally heavy rain during the afternoon and evening hours across central PA. Although vertical wind shear profiles were not particularly strong, strong destabilization lead to robust updrafts which produced marginally severe hail and damaging winds/wet microbursts. Localized flash flooding occurred in Potter and McKean Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Frugality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave crossing the region helped support for the development of showers and thunderstorms a few of which became severe. However, the bigger threat was heavy rain, as there was plenty of low level moisture to support one to two inch an hour rain rates. Once cell in particular produced 3+ inches of rain as it slowly moved southward across Venango, Indiana, Westmoreland, and Armstrong counties in Pennsylvania and Garrett county in Maryland. The towns along the Kiskiminetas River; Apollo, Vandergrift, and Gilpin were hit particularly hard by flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported road and basement flooding in Chicora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a series of thunderstorms with heavy downpours in southeastern Pennsylvania mainly during the morning and early afternoon of the 1st. Event totals reached 1 to 2 inches and caused some poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused some poor drainage flooding along Montgomery Avenue in West Chester. One vehicle became disabled in the flood waters.  Event precipitation totals in West Chester were 1.86 inches and 1.35 inches respectively. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late morning over western Pennsylvania and intensified into a well-defined squall line /QLCS/ with bowing segments as it moved through central Pennsylvania during the afternoon. The line of storms produced corridors of severe straight-line winds which prompted numerous reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along Brick Hill Road near Orwigsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late morning over western Pennsylvania and intensified into a well-defined squall line /QLCS/ with bowing segments as it moved through central Pennsylvania during the afternoon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees on I-83 north of the Rt 30 interchange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms produced both wind damage and flash flooding in Berks County and southeastern Pennsylvania on the 13th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates averaged 2.5 to 7.0 inches, highest in Chester and Montgomery Counties. In addition to widespread poor drainage flooding, flooding also affected sections of the Brandywine and Wissahickon basins.  Major flooding occurred along sections of the Wissahickon and Valley Creeks. Vehicle water rescues were performed in Chester, Montgomery and lower Bucks Counties. The AAA of Mid-Atlantic reported a 60 percent increase in assistance calls. Because the torrential rain coincided with the morning commute, traffic on some interstates were backed up for hours. SEPTA commuter regional rail lines experienced on average thirty minute system-wide delays through the morning.  Arrival delays to Philadelphia International Airport reached 90 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the morning heavy rain on the 13th continued flooding along some of the creeks in eastern Chester County into the afternoon and night, especially in the Brandywine Basin. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek below Downingtown had moderate flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage from 725 a.m. EDT through 413 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 10.78 feet at 1045 a.m. EDT.  The main stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford also had moderate flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 2 p.m. EDT on the 13th through 237 a.m. EDT on the 14th. It crested at 11.72 feet at 815 p.m. EDT on the 13th.  The Valley Creek at Valley Forge had major flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage from 705 a.m. EDT through 214 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 12.82 feet at 945 a.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 6.53 inches in Coatesville, 4.24 inches in Chesterbrook, 4.15 inches in Downingtown, 3.73 inches in Tredyfrrin, 3.14 inches in Unionville and 2.65 inches in West Chester.

Once cell in particular produced 3+ inches of rain as it slowly moved southward across Venango, Indiana, Westmoreland, and Armstrong counties in Pennsylvania and Garrett county in Maryland. The towns along the Kiskiminetas River; Apollo, Vandergrift, and Gilpin were hit particularly hard by flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported that Back Creek Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective complex of showers and strong to severe thunderstorms trailing a northward advancing warm front caused wind damage and flash flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania the morning of the 13th. About 6,000 PECO Energy homes and businesses lost power, mainly in Chester and Delaware Counties. The power was restored by the end of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in and around Radnor Township in northern Delaware County.

Once cell in particular produced 3+ inches of rain as it slowly moved southward across Venango, Indiana, Westmoreland, and Armstrong counties in Pennsylvania and Garrett county in Maryland. The towns along the Kiskiminetas River; Apollo, Vandergrift, and Gilpin were hit particularly hard by flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported several inches of water on Route 22 in Blairsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective complex of showers and strong to severe thunderstorms trailing a northward advancing warm front caused wind damage and flash flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania the morning of the 13th. About 6,000 PECO Energy homes and businesses lost power, mainly in Chester and Delaware Counties. The power was restored by the end of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in and around Warminster Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm over northern Columbia County produced heavy rain and localized flash flooding during the early morning hours on the 2nd. The flooding was confined to the Sugarloaf Township/Benton area. Later that day, intense rainfall produced flash flooding in Northern Cambria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in the Sugarloaf Township/Benton and Stillwater areas.  A washout was reported on Beech Glen Road which blocked one lane of traffic.  Agricultural flooding was also reported, and a pumpkin patch reported significant losses due to the high water.  A small mudslide and downed trees occurred along a section of State Route 239. This caused the road to be restricted to a single lane until approximately 0400 EDT while PA D.O.T. cleared the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain early in the morning on the 2nd caused flooding in the Poconos. Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 3 inches across southeast Carbon County and western Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across southeast Carbon County and caused poor drainage flooding in Towamensing and Lower Towamensing Townships. Event precipitation totals included 2.56 inches in Weatherly and 1.22 inches in Beltzville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass over the Ohio Valley was broken up on the 10th by a shortwave trough and a surface cold front that came moved through during the afternoon hours. Storms developed ahead of the cold front around noon, and ended with the exit of the cold front through northwest Pennsylvania around 7 pm. Reports of severe weather were received from both Erie and Crawford Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was observed.  Some vehicles were damaged by the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms produced both wind damage and flash flooding in Berks County and southeastern Pennsylvania on the 13th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates averaged 2.5 to 7.0 inches, highest in Chester and Montgomery Counties. In addition to widespread poor drainage flooding, flooding also affected sections of the Brandywine and Wissahickon basins.  Major flooding occurred along sections of the Wissahickon and Valley Creeks. Vehicle water rescues were performed in Chester, Montgomery and lower Bucks Counties. The AAA of Mid-Atlantic reported a 60 percent increase in assistance calls. Because the torrential rain coincided with the morning commute, traffic on some interstates were backed up for hours. SEPTA commuter regional rail lines experienced on average thirty minute system-wide delays through the morning.  Arrival delays to Philadelphia International Airport reached 90 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of roadways and smaller streams in the southeastern part of Berks County.  Event precipitation totals included 2.82 inches in Reading, 2.66 inches in Wyomissing, 2.39 inches in Mohnton, 2.28 inches in Wernersville and 2.27 inches in Sinking Spring.

The heaviest precipitation for this event fell in Chester County.  About 30 vehicles became stuck in flood waters and five water rescues were performed. A mudslide occurred on the Pennsylvania Turnpike near mile marker 318 near Chester Springs. The Valley Creek at Valley Forge had major flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage from 705 a.m. EDT through 214 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 12.82 feet at 945 a.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 6.53 inches in Coatesville, 4.24 inches in Chesterbrook, 4.15 inches in Downingtown, 3.73 inches in Tredyfrrin, 3.14 inches in Unionville and 2.65 inches in West Chester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a slow moving cold front with the help of an old outflow boundary in the afternoon on the 7th. Better shear and instability over Pennsylvania allowed for a few storms to become severe with the main threat being hail.

Energy moving through the flow aloft helped to kick off thunderstorms with severe winds causing downed trees in Edinboro.  Multiple downed trees blocked one lane of Interstate 79 near Edinboro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported multiple trees down blocking one lane of Interstate 79 near Edinboro.

In addition, several residents on Glade Run Road had to evacuate their residences by boat. In total, over 200 homes sustained flood damage, mainly to basements.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 call center reported that a local grocery store, drug store, and bank were all flooded on Little Deer Creek Road in Curtisville. In addition, 3 people were stranded in a vehicle in floodwaters on Bullcreek Road and flooding was reported on Saxonburg Boulevard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of flooded roadways in Cowansville. In addition, Kittaning Hollow Road and Hile Road in Brady's Bend were flooded and the Meadow Lake Manor personal care home flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and damaged a home in Valley Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain early in the morning on the 2nd caused flooding in the Poconos. Event precipitation totals averaged 1 to 3 inches across southeast Carbon County and western Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across western Monroe County and caused poor drainage flooding in Polk, Jackson and Chestnuthill Townships. In Chestnuthill Township, flooding carried debris onto the intersection of Toll and Merwinsburg Roads.  Event precipitation totals included 2.66 in Kresgeville, 1.96 inches in Pocono Pines and 1.60 inches in Mount Pocono.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of rain with embedded thunderstorms caused flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and upper Bucks County and poor drainage flooding in Philadelphia as well as brief minor flooding along the Frankford Creek on the 28th. The rain started during the morning of the 28th, fell at its heaviest around Noon EDT and ended during the early evening of the 28th. Rainfall amounts varied across east central and southeast Pennsylvania, but averaged between 1 and 3 inches in most places. The precipitation formed and rode along a stationary front over Pennsylvania and New Jersey. This boundary started to sag southward during the late afternoon and night as a low pressure system that formed along the boundary passed south of Philadelphia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller creeks as well as poor drainage and roadway flooding from Allentown southward in Lehigh County. The heavy rain fell so quickly that manhole covers were popping. Roadway flooding, stranded vehicles and water rescues in Allentown were reported on Mack Boulevard, Basin and Auburn Streets, Elmo Street, Mohawk Street and South 4th, 8th and 12th Streets.  Farther to the south, Pennsylvania State Route 309 was flooded and closed in Upper Saucon Township. Event precipitation totals included 2.91 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.64 inches in Orefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing eastward from southern Ontario and western Lake Erie triggered scattered severe thunderstorms across central Pennsylvania during the mid-afternoon and early evening. The convective environment was characterized by cool air aloft associated with a shortwave trough and marginal deep-layer mean winds and shear. Despite the marginal mid-level flow/shear, daytime heating promoted steep lapse rates and ample instability which lead to several reports of large hail and isolated damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail (1 inch diameter) was reported southwest of Bradford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing eastward from southern Ontario and western Lake Erie triggered scattered severe thunderstorms across central Pennsylvania during the mid-afternoon and early evening. The convective environment was characterized by cool air aloft associated with a shortwave trough and marginal deep-layer mean winds and shear. Despite the marginal mid-level flow/shear, daytime heating promoted steep lapse rates and ample instability which lead to several reports of large hail and isolated damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail (1 inch diameter) was reported near New Holland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front moving east across the area on the night of August 12-13. A cluster of thunderstorms moved in off Lake Erie around 1230 AM with strong thunderstorm winds causing damage to trees. Heavy rainfall accompanied the thunderstorms. A tree fell on a tent in Mill Creek Township and the tent started to wash away. People had to be rescued but no injuries occurred. Power outages were also reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree fell on a tent in a campground in Mill Creek Township.  No injuries were reported.  The Department of Highways also reported large limbs and powerlines brought down by thunderstorm winds in Erie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal lee side trof coupled with unseasonably hot and humid weather helped trigger widely scattered strong to severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 19th. Some small hail also fell. Berks County was most affected by the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Spring Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms produced both wind damage and flash flooding in Berks County and southeastern Pennsylvania on the 13th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates averaged 2.5 to 7.0 inches, highest in Chester and Montgomery Counties. In addition to widespread poor drainage flooding, flooding also affected sections of the Brandywine and Wissahickon basins.  Major flooding occurred along sections of the Wissahickon and Valley Creeks. Vehicle water rescues were performed in Chester, Montgomery and lower Bucks Counties. The AAA of Mid-Atlantic reported a 60 percent increase in assistance calls. Because the torrential rain coincided with the morning commute, traffic on some interstates were backed up for hours. SEPTA commuter regional rail lines experienced on average thirty minute system-wide delays through the morning.  Arrival delays to Philadelphia International Airport reached 90 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in western Bucks County.  Vehicle rescues were performed in Southampton. The Little Neshaminy Creek at Neshaminy was above its 8 foot flood stage from 1029 a.m. EDT through 153 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 8.98 feet at Noon EDT. Event precipitation totals included 2.86 inches in Penndel, 2.17 inches in Warrington and 1.94 inches in Quakertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave crossing the region helped support for the development of showers and thunderstorms a few of which became severe. However, the bigger threat was heavy rain, as there was plenty of low level moisture to support one to two inch an hour rain rates. Once cell in particular produced 3+ inches of rain as it slowly moved southward across Venango, Indiana, Westmoreland, and Armstrong counties in Pennsylvania and Garrett county in Maryland. The towns along the Kiskiminetas River; Apollo, Vandergrift, and Gilpin were hit particularly hard by flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local observer reported 2.31 inches of rain in the past 4 1/2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms in southeastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 20th. This front also ended the longest and most oppressive hot spell of the summer of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn Spotter estimated wind gusts to severe levels during a thunderstorm in Brookhaven. Several large tree limbs were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the afternoon thunderstorms that formed near the stationary frontal boundary developed a funnel cloud over Berks County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud formed during a thunderstorm over Greenwich Township. It did not touch down, there were no reports of wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms produced both wind damage and flash flooding in Berks County and southeastern Pennsylvania on the 13th. In addition to widespread poor drainage flooding, flooding also affected sections of the Brandywine and Wissahickon basins.  Major flooding occurred along sections of the Wissahickon and Valley Creeks. Vehicle water rescues were performed in Chester, Montgomery and lower Bucks Counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage flash flooding as well as creek flooding within Philadelphia.  Flooding occurred along the Frankford, Pennypack and Wissahickon Creeks. A teenager was rescued from the Pennypack Creek in the Holmesburg section of the city. Extensive flooding snarled traffic along the Schuylkill Expressway. Lincoln Drive along the Wissahickon Creek was flooded in both directions and closed.  The Wissahickon Environmental Center Camp was cancelled because of flooding. The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue had moderate flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage twice from 820 a.m. EDT through 1011 a.m. EDT on the 13th. Its highest crest was 8.85 feet at 830 a.m. EDT. The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge crested at 8.87 feet at 345 p.m. EDT.  The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth crested at 7.23 feet at 415 p.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 2.20 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.93 inches in Northeast Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant instability combined with modest lapse rates and abundant deep-layer moisture (PW) produced numerous thunderstorm clusters with locally heavy rain during the afternoon and evening hours across central PA. Although vertical wind shear profiles were not particularly strong, strong destabilization lead to robust updrafts which produced marginally severe hail and damaging winds/wet microbursts. Localized flash flooding occurred in Potter and McKean Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Renovo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the morning heavy rain on the 13th continued flooding along some of the waterways in Philadelphia into the evening of the 13th. This was especially true of creeks whose sources are in Montgomery County as heavier rain fell in their headwater regions. The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 848 a.m. EDT through 511 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 8.87 feet at 345 p.m. EDT. The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth within Philadelphia had moderate flooding and was above its 5 foot flood stage from 956 a.m. EDT through 801 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 7.23 feet at 415 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 2.20 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.93 inches in Northeast Philadelphia.

The Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford had moderate flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 2 p.m. EDT on the 13th through 237 a.m. EDT on the 14th. It crested at 11.72 feet at 815 p.m. EDT on the 13th. Event precipitation totals included 3.10 inches in Wayne, 2.50 inches in Newtown Square and 1.72 inches in Aston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms preceded a cold frontal passage during the late morning and first half of the afternoon in Eastern Pennsylvania. The strong southerly flow preceding the squall line knocked down a tree in Reading (Berks County). The tree fell along North Fourth Street and damaged a home and a non functioning streetlight.  About 25 Metropolitan Edison customers lost power because of the downed tree. The same strong southerly flow preceding this front coincided with the spring tides associated with the new moon to produce some minor tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River during the afternoon high tide cycle on the 7th. High tide reached 8.40 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia, minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of severe thunderstorms knocked down a few trees in the western part of Berks County centered around Upper Bern Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves diving through a broad upper level trough brought rounds of showers and thunderstorms through the last several days of June. With a weakly capped atmosphere, a cold pool associated with two such upper level waves helped to spark two areas of convection in the afternoon on June 28th. There were numerous reports of wind damage across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. In addition, training thunderstorms produced several areas of flooding, including some flash flooding across several counties in western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
A shortwave trough and a slow moving cold front moving across the region brought rounds of heavy rain to portions of eastern Ohio and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported flooding along Ash street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and a slow moving cold front moving across the region brought rounds of heavy rain to portions of eastern Ohio and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported flooding on Locust Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia in a hot and humid air mass with weak upper level winds. A few storms became severe, and heavy rainfall produced some flash flooding and areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported trees and wires down on Marion Avenue in Forest Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey confirmed a EF1 tornado just west of the town of Moravia in Lawrence county. The tornado started in a field where it was captured on camera and video by persons traveling on State Route 168. It then crossed State Route 168 and destroyed 2 barns. A gravity wagon was flipped over in a nearby field. The tornado then moved across a soybean field onto Halltown Road snapping and uprooting several trees. The tornado then dissipated. Maximum winds were estimated at 90 MPH, with a path length of just over one mile. The maximum path width was around 250 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms tied to a surface front  dropped several inches of rain over northwest Pennsylvania the morning of the 27th. Particularly hit hard were Jefferson and adjacent Clearfield counties, were roads were made impassable by over four to six inches of rainfall. The local EMA Director stated there were 49 water rescues and 50 people were placed in shelters. The hardest hit areas include Punxsutawney, Sykesville, Stump Creek, Big Run. and Reynoldsville. Preliminary damage reports have a total of 347 homes and 34 businesses damaged by flood waters. Of those, nine homes were destroyed and three businesses were a total loss. Damage is already estimated to be about $3 million but could be upwards of $5 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported that State Route 219 at Old Gray Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A belt of seasonably strong lower-mid tropospheric flow in the Ohio Valley on the eastern periphery of a large-scale upper trough impinged on a moist and unstable environment over central Pennsylvania and resulted in severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Multicell thunderstorm clusters organized into mini bowing line segments which produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Benner Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving towards the Great Lakes helped to push a strong cold front across the Ohio Valley in the early morning hours of the 22nd. A shallow convective line of thunderstorms developed along the front, providing for some strong to severe wind gusts. Damage was reported in several locations across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service employee reported a large tree down across State Route 30, 1 mile east of Route 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds and hail across southeastern Lancaster County between 16-1700 EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph blew down a tobacco shed on the 400 block of Paradise Lane in Paradise Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding in Berks County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached two to three inches in sections of the county.   Extremely wet antecedent conditions made it easier for flash flooding to occur. In fact, it was the wettest June on record in Philadelphia (10.56 inches)  and the 6th wettest June on record in Allentown (6.83 inches). On a county weighted average, June monthly precipitation ranged from 6.9 inches in Berks County to 10.4 inches in Chester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of small creeks (including Willow Creek) as well as roadway flooding (including Main Street) in Fleetwood. Event precipitation total in Fleetwood was 2.96 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms intensified over northeast West Virginia and tracked northeastward across central Pennsylvania during the mid/late afternoon hours. Strong wind fields aloft and moderate instability promoted numerous reports of damaging winds across a large portion of the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down utility wires in Centre Hall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary boundary remained the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms again on June 30th. While shear and instability remained meager, with cape values of 1000j/kg or less, some storms were able to support themselves to severe levels. A little more than a dozen wind damage and hail reports combined made up the afternoon. But, once again, training thunderstorms produced flooding problems, this time over Allegheny county in Pennsylvania.

Training bands of locally heavy rain impacted parts of the middle Susquehanna Valley during the late morning and early afternoon. A deep east-southeast flow from the Atlantic pulled high moisture into the region around an upper-level low rotating northward from MD/VA. Rainfall rates of 1-2/hour were common with localized rainfall rates as high as 4/hour. Several locations around the Lewisburg, Selinsgrove and Sunbury areas picked up between 2-3 inches of rain over a short duration. Urbanization combined with the intense rains lead to flash flooding along SR 11 and the closure of Reagan/Hamilton underpasses near Sunbury. Small streams including Bull Run and Turtle Creek also came out of their banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and flash flooding closed the Reagan and Hamilton underpasses near Sunbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe squall line/bowing MCS moved southeast across central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening hours. This organized convective system produced widespread damaging winds across the CTP CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Refton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm air mass just south of a slowly moving cold front combined to produce strong to severe thunderstorms mainly across the Poconos and Lehigh Valley during the late afternoon and early evening of the 10th. The severe thunderstorms that did occur produced both large hail and damaging winds. About 400 Pennsylvania Power and Light customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Whitemarsh Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced, narrow line of heavy showers raced eastward across the area during the late evening and early overnight hours. Despite limited instability and no lightning detected with the shallow convection, the presence of an intense wind field in excess of 50 knots just above the surface promoted corridors of wind damage with the passage of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers with estimated winds near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires near Gettysburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front that set up just north of I-80 became the focus for convection on the afternoon of April 10th as a shortwave riding along the front approached. Several lines of showers and thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail as they sank southeastward across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported trees down in Barnett Township on Route 899 and River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front that set up just north of I-80 became the focus for convection on the afternoon of April 10th as a shortwave riding along the front approached. Several lines of showers and thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail as they sank southeastward across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous trees, poles, and wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms intensified over northeast West Virginia and tracked northeastward across central Pennsylvania during the mid/late afternoon hours. Strong wind fields aloft and moderate instability promoted numerous reports of damaging winds across a large portion of the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires near Hughesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity during the afternoon as large scale lift was enhanced by a mid-level disturbance pivoting around an upper-level low centered over southwestern Ontario. The combination of enhanced lift, strong instability and deep layer wind profiles supported organized storm structures including bowing line segments that produced areas of damaging winds and severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail (1 inch diameter) was reported in Clearville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed across western Pennsylvania in the late afternoon of the 10th well ahead of a cold front crossing Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey showed a narrow swath of sporadic tree damage from near Airport Road and State Route 8 to near Mushrush Road and Winters Road. Damage included a large tree uprooted along State Route 8, along with downed limbs and small trees snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed across western Pennsylvania in the late afternoon of the 10th well ahead of a cold front crossing Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down in Uniontown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia in a hot and humid air mass with weak upper level winds. A few storms became severe, and heavy rainfall produced some flash flooding and areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported flash flooding with a swamped vehicle, as well as water flowing into a business.

Despite limited instability and no lightning detected with the shallow convection, the presence of an intense wind field in excess of 50 knots just above the surface promoted corridors of wind damage with the passage of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers with estimated winds near 60 mph knocked down trees along RT 160 near Berlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced, narrow line of heavy showers raced eastward across the area during the predawn and early morning hours. Despite limited instability and no lightning detected with the shallow convection, the presence of an intense wind field in excess of 50 knots just above the surface promoted corridors of wind damage with the passage of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers with estimated winds near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires in Bedford. The downed trees resulted in minor structural damage to some residential homes.

This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm clusters across western Pennsylvania organized into bowing line segments as they moved east-southeast across the southern Alleghenies and Laurel Highlands during the late afternoon and early evening. The line of storms produced isolated wind damage and sub-severe hail. A more discrete thunderstorm cell just ahead of the main line of storms produced a brief, weak EF0 tornado in Somerset County. An area of locally heavy rain produced flash flooding in York County during the late evening, with areal flooding persisting into the early morning on 8/8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorm rains produced flash flooding across portions of southern York County during the late evening and early overnight hours. Spotter reports indicated  two inches of rain fell in about 1 hour. Street flooding was reported in Hanover. A water rescue was conducted at the intersection of Seven Valleys Road (State Route 616) and Maple Street in North Codorus Township. A motorist drove her car into Codorus Creek which has flooded over the road. Emergency responders were able to rescue both occupants of the stranded vehicle. This section of the road along Codorus Creek was closed for much of the night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper air disturbance coupled with a weak surface trof and hot and humid weather developed a line of strong to isolated severe thunderstorms in southeast Pennsylvania during the early evening of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust front and a line of strong thunderstorms knocked down several trees and caused numerous power outages in Abington, Glenside, and nearby areas at 7:47 p.m. EDT on the evening of the 7th.  PECO reported approximately 4,500 customers without power, with 4,463 of those outages being reported in Abington and Glenside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and moist air mass for early September helped scattered thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon and evening. Isolated flooding and wind damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported Goldscheitter Road closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense, north-south oriented squall line accompanied a strong cold front across central and eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening hours. The strongly forced low-topped convection (with little to no lightning) developed in an environment characterized by weak instability and high shear. Mini bowing segments/LEWPs embedded within the squall line produced numerous areas of wind damage across the mid-lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down 8 large pine trees in Orangeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong south to southwest winds preceding a cold frontal passage brought an unseasonably mild and moist air mass into Eastern Pennsylvania. This caused heavy rain to fall and caused poor drainage flooding as well as stream and river flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania on the 31st.  Most of the waterway flooding was minor and all creeks and  rivers were back within bankfull by the evening of the 31st. Event totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches. The rain began during the second half of the afternoon on the 30th and fell at its heaviest during the early morning of the 31st as convective elements moved through eastern Pennsylvania. The rain ended around 7 a.m. EST on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth was above its 5 foot flood stage from 945 a.m. EST through 1219 p.m. EST on the 31st. It crested at 5.23 feet at 1115 a.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 1.28 inches in Wyndmoor, 1.18 inches in Rockledge and 0.94 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport. Heavier rain fell in upstream areas of the Wissahickon Basin in Montgomery County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed in conjunction with a strengthening surface low over northern Indiana propagated westward towards the region late on the 12th. Out ahead of this system, strong speed shear and instability contributed to supercellular development along a warm frontal boundary that bisected the region. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were reported before the bow echo reached eastern Ohio very early on the 13th. This bow echo, with a history of widespread wind damage, then crossed through the forecast area through 6am, causing several reports of downed trees and wires through much of eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and southwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fayette County emergency management director reported a 1/2 to 1 mile swath of trees down near Farmington in the area of State Route 381 and Rankin Road. The director stated the damage was due to straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front and upper level trough pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 10th producing widespread severe weather and flash flooding from eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. This included 3 separate tornadoes. One tornado occurred in Lawrence county, PA, with 2 additional tornadoes across portions of Tuscarawas and Carroll counties in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported trees down.
